proposals.title: A Dual-Framework Analysis of Lock-In and Rebound: Measuring Key Drivers of Unsustainable Transport and Energy Development
proposals.motivation: Despite global climate commitments, progress in decoupling economic growth from transport and energy-related carbon emissions remains insufficient. Pre-2026 literature identifies systemic barriers, but lacks a unified framework to measure the relative strength of different 'unsustainable development drivers'—such as infrastructural lock-in and behavioral rebound effects—that prevent nations from meeting critical decarbonization thresholds. This gap hinders targeted policy intervention.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a dual framework categorizing drivers as either 'structural-institutional lock-in' or 'behavioral-economic rebound' will reveal distinct, measurable patterns of influence that explain national deviations from sustainable development thresholds in transport and residential energy use.
proposals.method: Building on Banister's prior work on decoupling and feedback exercises, we will develop a two-stage analytical model. First, we will construct a composite indicator system for each driver category using pre-2026 OECD and Eurostat data (e.g., transport modal share, fuel prices, housing stock, appliance ownership). Second, we will employ a panel data regression analysis with fixed effects, similar to the approach in the 'Tax or Clean Technology?' paper, to quantify the impact of each driver category on carbon emissions per capita across a sample of 30 OECD countries from 2000-2023, controlling for GDP and technological diffusion.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a novel, measurable taxonomy of unsustainable development drivers, moving beyond qualitative description. It will offer policymakers a diagnostic tool to identify whether lock-in or rebound effects are the primary barrier in specific national contexts, enabling more precise interventions in transport and household energy demand management.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in synthesizing concepts of infrastructural lock-in from transport studies with behavioral rebound insights from energy feedback research into a single, testable dual framework, and applying it to explain threshold compliance at the national scale.

proposals.title: Capability Maintenance in Low-Carbon Transitions: A Mixed-Methods Analysis of Enablers for Sustained Mobility and Social Connection
proposals.motivation: Transport research, particularly on decoupling travel from emissions, often frames reduced mobility as a loss or constraint, potentially overlooking the social and well-being consequences. A pre-2026 policy focus on carbon reduction risks neglecting the capabilities required to maintain essential social participation and access, especially for non-drivers or those in energy-poor households. This study addresses a critical gap in understanding how to sustain mobility capabilities within planetary boundaries.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that maintained mobility capability and social connection during travel behaviour change are primarily enabled by a synergistic combination of proximate local amenities, accessible active travel infrastructure, and integrated digital-social feedback systems, rather than by individual resources alone.
proposals.method: The research employs a sequential mixed-methods design. First, a quantitative analysis of UK National Travel Survey data will use structural equation modelling to identify clusters of individuals maintaining high social connectivity despite low motorized travel, testing associations with built environment variables. Second, building on Banister's prior feedback exercise methodology, a tailored intervention with 50 households will use detailed activity-travel diaries and real-time feedback on energy and travel carbon footprints, combined with semi-structured interviews, to qualitatively explore the enablers and barriers to maintaining social capabilities.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel 'capability maintenance' framework to transport and energy studies, shifting the analytical lens from loss to enablement. It will provide empirical evidence on the socio-technical configurations that support low-carbon, high-wellbeing lifestyles, offering concrete insights for integrated urban and transport policy aimed at just sustainability transitions.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in inverting the dominant 'mobility loss' narrative in decarbonisation research and explicitly linking the concepts of travel feedback, built environment analysis, and social capability—a synthesis not yet fully explored in pre-2026 literature on decoupling.

proposals.title: Decoupling through Activity Integration: A Multi-Level Analysis of Transport's Embedded Role in Carbon-Intensive Daily Practices
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 climate policy and transport research often treat mobility as a distinct sector for emissions reduction, focusing on vehicle technology or modal shift. This siloed approach risks overlooking how transport demand is fundamentally derived from, and locked into, broader social practices and activity patterns (e.g., work, care, leisure). Understanding these embedded relationships is critical for achieving deep and durable decoupling of well-being from carbon-intensive travel.
proposals.hypothesis: The carbon intensity of personal travel is not primarily determined by transport choices in isolation, but by the configuration and spatio-temporal fixity of the daily activities that necessitate travel. Interventions targeting activity patterns will yield greater and more equitable emissions reductions than those targeting transport modes alone.
proposals.method: The research will employ a mixed-methods, multi-level design. First, a practice-theory informed survey will quantify the linkage between specific activity bundles (e.g., 'the working parent routine') and associated travel energy use. Second, building on Banister's prior feedback exercise methods, we will conduct a longitudinal, digitally-augmented diary study with sensor and self-report data to capture the real-time interplay between activity scheduling, social coordination, and travel outcomes. Analysis will use structural equation modelling to test pathways from social practices to travel energy, and sequence alignment algorithms to classify and compare carbon-intensive daily routines.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel framework for evaluating transport and carbon policy not by modal share, but by its capacity to reconfigure underlying activity systems. It will generate empirical evidence on which daily practices are most carbon-locked, offering a new target for behavioural and urban planning interventions aimed at 'avoid' and 'shift' strategies for sustainable mobility.
proposals.novelty: While practice theory has been applied to energy use, its systematic integration with high-resolution travel energy data and sequence analysis to deconstruct the 'black box' of daily routine is novel. It moves beyond studying transport choices to modelling the generative activity structures from which travel demand emerges.

proposals.title: Decoupling Well-being from Carbon: An Activity-Based Assessment of Transport, Daily Activity Sequences, and Quality of Life for an Ageing Population
proposals.motivation: Ageing populations face mobility challenges that can reduce quality of life (QoL) and increase social exclusion, while transport policy must also achieve deep decarbonisation. Pre-2026 research on decoupling often focuses on aggregate energy or emissions from travel, neglecting how low-carbon transport configurations (e.g., walkable neighborhoods, reduced car access) enable or constrain the daily activity patterns that constitute QoL for vulnerable groups like the elderly.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesise that for elderly populations, specific low-carbon transport system configurations (prioritising walking, public transport, and mobility-as-a-service) can support activity sequences that sustain or enhance QoL, thereby achieving a 'double dividend' of well-being and emissions reduction.
proposals.method: We will employ an activity-based transport model (ABM), using pre-2026 frameworks like ALBATROSS or a custom MATSim agent-based simulation, to generate synthetic daily activity-travel patterns for elderly agents under different policy scenarios. Agent utility functions will integrate traditional travel impedance with QoL metrics derived from activity sequence completeness, social participation, and access to services. The model will be calibrated and validated using UK time-use and travel survey data (e.g., UKHLS, NTS), with scenario analysis comparing carbon emissions and simulated QoL outcomes.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, granular methodology to evaluate the equity and well-being impacts of decarbonisation policies, moving beyond aggregate emissions. It will contribute to transport and ageing policy by identifying specific interventions that simultaneously support elderly QoL and carbon reduction goals.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in fusing detailed activity-based transport modeling, a core transport planning tool, with a structured assessment of QoL for the elderly, directly linking micro-level daily activity patterns to macro-level decarbonisation and well-being policy objectives.

proposals.title: Linking Real-Time Mobility, Mood, and Carbon Impact: An Ecological Momentary Assessment Study of Older Adults' Local Travel
proposals.motivation: Decoupling personal mobility from carbon emissions is critical for climate goals, yet policies often overlook the experiential and well-being dimensions of sustainable travel, especially for older adults. Understanding the immediate emotional and practical context of trips, particularly walking, is essential for designing low-carbon transport systems that are adopted and sustained, rather than merely technically feasible.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that for older adults, (a) walking trips are associated with more positive real-time mood and experiential outcomes compared to motorized trips, and (b) these positive experiences are a stronger predictor of subsequent walking behavior than general environmental attitudes, offering a key pathway for behavioral carbon mitigation.
proposals.method: We will employ a smartphone-based Ecological Momentary Assessment (EMA) protocol with 100 participants aged 65+. Over two weeks, participants will receive 5 random prompts daily to report mood, location, and trip purpose. Concurrently, a triggered survey will activate post-trip to capture mode choice, experience, and trip attributes. Data will be analyzed using multilevel modeling to examine within-person associations between travel mode, mood, and context. Trip distances (via GPS) will be coupled with standard emission factors to estimate individual carbon impacts.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide novel empirical evidence on the micro-level, psychological drivers of low-carbon travel behavior in an understudied demographic. It will extend decoupling research by integrating real-time experiential data with carbon accounting, moving beyond aggregate analyses to understand the behavioral mechanisms of emission reduction.
proposals.novelty: While EMA is established in health psychology, its application to directly link real-time travel experiences with carbon outcomes in transport studies is novel. This bridges Banister's prior work on feedback and behavior with fine-grained psychological measurement, offering a new lens on the 'soft' factors in decarbonization.

proposals.title: Beyond the Elderly: Applying a Quality-of-Life Framework to Transport Accessibility for Younger Adults with Mobility Constraints
proposals.motivation: Transport research on accessibility and quality of life (QoL) has heavily focused on the elderly, often overlooking younger adults with permanent disabilities or temporary mobility limitations (e.g., due to injury or illness). This gap is critical as these groups face distinct life-stage challenges related to employment, education, and social participation. Understanding their transport needs is essential for designing equitable, low-carbon urban systems that genuinely decouple mobility from carbon emissions and energy use for all citizens.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that for younger adults with mobility constraints, perceived QoL is more strongly correlated with access to flexible, on-demand transport modes and digital connectivity than with conventional mobility metrics, and that interventions targeting these factors can significantly reduce associated transport-related carbon footprints by substituting for high-emission, dependency-based trips.
proposals.method: Building on Banister's prior feedback exercise methodology, we will conduct a mixed-methods study. First, a targeted survey and activity-travel diary will be administered to a stratified sample of younger adults (18-64) with self-reported mobility constraints. Second, we will implement an innovative feedback exercise using personalized dashboards that visualize participants' travel patterns, estimated carbon emissions, and energy use against benchmarks, incorporating real-time suggestions for active travel (walking/wheeling) where feasible. Data analysis will employ structural equation modelling (SEM) to test the relationships between constraint type, transport mode use, perceived accessibility, QoL indicators, and calculated carbon emissions.
proposals.contribution: This research will extend the decoupling and QoL framework to a neglected demographic, providing empirical evidence on the travel behaviour and emissions profile of this group. It will contribute a novel methodology for engaging users with mobility constraints in behaviour change, offering evidence-based policy insights for inclusive, low-carbon transport planning that moves beyond a one-size-fits-all approach.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in explicitly transferring the QoL and feedback framework—previously applied to households or the elderly—to the under-researched group of non-elderly with mobility constraints, and directly linking their accessibility needs to carbon emission mitigation strategies within the transport decoupling agenda.

proposals.title: Beyond the Parking Space: A Comparative Analysis of Street Redesign and Parking Policy for Decoupling Urban Transport from Carbon Emissions
proposals.motivation: Urban transport policy has long focused on parking management as a primary tool for managing demand and reducing emissions, a focus evident in pre-2026 literature. However, this may overlook more direct interventions that reshape the physical and experiential fabric of streets to inherently promote low-carbon modes like walking. A systematic comparison is needed to challenge this established policy hierarchy and identify the most effective pathways for decoupling travel from carbon.
proposals.hypothesis: Direct street redesign interventions (e.g., pedestrianisation, protected cycle lanes, traffic calming) are more effective than parking policy adjustments (e.g., pricing, supply reduction) at reducing vehicle kilometres travelled (VKT) and associated carbon emissions, primarily by inducing a more significant and lasting modal shift towards active travel.
proposals.method: The research will employ a quasi-experimental, longitudinal panel study design, comparing 'treatment' and 'control' neighbourhoods in a UK city. It will integrate pre-2026 methods: 1) High-resolution energy and emissions modelling using the ASIF framework to calculate carbon impacts from travel data. 2) A 'different' feedback mechanism, inspired by Banister's prior work, providing households with personalised data on emissions and travel time savings from using redesigned streets. 3) Statistical analysis, including difference-in-differences and structural equation modelling, to isolate the causal effect of each intervention type on VKT, mode share, and perceived accessibility.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide robust, empirical evidence on the relative efficacy of two major policy levers, moving beyond theoretical debates. It will contribute to the decoupling literature by quantifying the carbon reduction potential of proactive street design versus reactive parking management. The findings will offer actionable insights for policymakers seeking the most direct route to low-carbon urban mobility.
proposals.novelty: While parking and street design are both studied, pre-2026 research rarely pits them against each other in a controlled, comparative framework that measures their true effect on carbon via behavioural change. The integration of a tailored feedback exercise within a physical intervention study creates a novel hybrid methodology to understand behavioural mechanisms.

proposals.title: Beyond the Feedback Loop: Testing the Long-Term Efficacy of Personal Travel Information on Mobility Choice and Carbon Decoupling
proposals.motivation: A core assumption in sustainable transport policy is that providing individuals with personalised feedback on their travel energy use and carbon emissions will induce pro-environmental behaviour change. However, evidence for sustained, long-term change is weak, risking ineffective policy. This research directly tests this foundational assumption, crucial for designing interventions that genuinely contribute to transport decarbonisation.
proposals.hypothesis: Providing personalised, quantified travel energy and carbon feedback leads to no statistically significant long-term change in modal choice, trip frequency, or distance travelled compared to a control group. Any observed short-term effects will decay over a 12-month period.
proposals.method: We will conduct a longitudinal, randomised controlled trial with 500 households, using GPS-enabled smartphones and travel diaries to collect baseline mobility data for one month. The treatment group will then receive a tailored monthly report for one year, detailing their travel carbon footprint, energy use, and comparisons to benchmarks, delivered via a dedicated app and postal summary. The control group receives no feedback. Data analysis will employ difference-in-differences models and survival analysis (Cox proportional hazards) to test for and model the persistence of any behavioural changes, controlling for socio-demographics and built environment factors.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide robust, longitudinal evidence on the causal link between information provision and travel behaviour, moving beyond short-term pilot studies. It will contribute directly to theories of behavioural change in transport and offer critical evidence for policymakers on the likely efficacy of information-based carbon mitigation strategies.
proposals.novelty: While feedback studies exist, few employ a rigorous long-term RCT design to explicitly test the decay of effects. The novelty lies in directly challenging a core, often untested, policy assumption and applying survival analysis to model the 'failure' (reversion) of pro-environmental travel behaviours over time.

proposals.title: Personalised Mobility Feedback Systems: A Randomised Controlled Trial to Decouple Travel Demand from Carbon Emissions
proposals.motivation: Achieving deep decarbonisation in transport requires not only technological shifts but also behavioural change to reduce overall travel demand and modal shift. Pre-2026 research, including Banister's work on decoupling and feedback exercises, shows that generic information is often ineffective. There is a critical need for targeted, personalised systems that make low-carbon mobility choices more salient and actionable for individuals.
proposals.hypothesis: That a personalised information system, which integrates real-time multi-modal travel data with individual carbon budgets and contextual trip constraints, will significantly increase the adoption of walking and low-carbon travel modes and reduce total travel-related carbon emissions compared to a control group receiving standard information.
proposals.method: We will design and deploy a smartphone-based system for a 6-month RCT with 500 households. The system will use a rule-based algorithm to analyse trip diaries and GPS data, classifying trips by purpose and mode. A personal carbon budget will be allocated based on historical data. The core feedback engine will employ a cost-comparison algorithm, presenting personalised, real-time alternatives for planned trips that optimise for carbon, time, and cost, with a specific emphasis on viable walking options. The architecture will integrate open transport APIs (e.g., GTFS) and a secure back-end for data processing and feedback delivery.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide robust, causal evidence on the efficacy of deeply personalised information for mobility behaviour change. It will advance methodological integration of energy and travel feedback, moving beyond electricity or single-mode studies. The findings will offer concrete design principles for scalable policy instruments aimed at demand-side decoupling of transport from emissions.
proposals.novelty: While feedback systems exist, this proposal's novelty lies in the integration of a dynamic personal carbon budget with a trip-specific alternative suggestion engine that prioritises contextual feasibility, directly testing the 'missing link' between information provision and sustained behavioural shift in a multi-modal travel environment.

proposals.title: Codifying Urban Parking Policy for Decoupling: A Systematic Evaluation of Good Practices from the Interreg IIIC City Parking Project
proposals.motivation: Urban parking policy is a critical yet under-formalized lever for achieving transport-related carbon emissions reductions and decoupling mobility growth from environmental impact. The Interreg IIIC City Parking project generated a rich but disparate set of European case studies, yet their collective lessons for effective carbon mitigation remain uncodified. Systematically evaluating these cases addresses a key gap in translating local policy experiments into generalizable, evidence-based strategies for sustainable transport.
proposals.hypothesis: That a systematic codification and comparative analysis of the City Parking project cases will reveal a core set of transferable good practices, and that the effectiveness of these practices in contributing to transport carbon decoupling is contingent on specific local contextual factors related to urban form, governance, and travel behavior.
proposals.method: The research will employ a mixed-methods approach. First, a structured content analysis will be conducted on all project documentation to extract policy measures, implementation contexts, and reported outcomes. Second, these elements will be formalized into a typology matrix, enabling cross-case comparison using Qualitative Comparative Analysis (QCA) to identify necessary and sufficient conditions for success. Third, selected cases will be subjected to a retrospective energy and carbon assessment, applying a simplified consumption-based accounting framework to estimate travel behavior changes and associated emission impacts.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a formalized, evidence-based typology of effective parking management practices for carbon mitigation, directly contributing to policy mobility in sustainable urban transport. It will advance methodological integration in transport studies by combining policy analysis with energy/emissions accounting, offering a replicable model for evaluating the carbon consequences of soft policy measures.
proposals.novelty: While parking policy is widely studied, this research uniquely applies systematic codification and configurational analysis (QCA) to a pre-existing, transnational set of implemented cases to derive generalizable principles for decoupling. It innovatively links qualitative policy evaluation with quantitative energy/emissions assessment at the project case level, a methodologically distinct contribution to Banister's ongoing work on feedback between behavior and resource use.

proposals.title: A Practical Framework for Integrating Land Use and Mobility Planning to Achieve Decoupling: A Feedback-Based Modelling Approach
proposals.motivation: Urban transport is a major and growing source of carbon emissions, yet planning for land use and mobility often proceeds in silos, undermining decarbonisation goals. Achieving meaningful decoupling of transport activity from emissions requires integrated planning that actively promotes low-energy modes like walking and reduces travel demand. Pre-2026 literature highlights this integration as a critical but under-implemented strategy for sustainable urban futures.
proposals.hypothesis: That a practical, feedback-driven integrated land use and transport (ILUT) modelling framework, which explicitly incorporates behavioural responses and energy/emissions outcomes, will demonstrate significantly greater potential for carbon mitigation than conventional, sequential planning approaches.
proposals.method: The research will develop and test a novel ILUT framework by coupling an agent-based transport model (using MATSim or similar) with a spatial interaction land use model. A key innovation is the integration of a feedback module, inspired by Banister's prior work, that simulates how changes in accessibility (from transport interventions) influence location choices and, conversely, how land use density (from planning policies) affects mode choice and travel distances. The framework will be calibrated and applied to a UK case study city, using pre-2026 data, to run scenarios comparing 'integrated' vs. 'business-as-usual' planning.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a transferable, practical modelling tool for planners to evaluate the synergistic carbon reduction benefits of combined land use and transport policies. It will advance the theoretical understanding of decoupling in urban systems by quantifying the 'co-benefits' of integration, directly contributing to evidence-based policy on low-carbon cities.
proposals.novelty: While ILUT models exist, this framework's novelty lies in its explicit design for practical policy testing and its core focus on simulating the feedback mechanisms between planning decisions, travel behaviour, and carbon emissions—a direct extension of Banister's work on behavioural feedback and decoupling.

proposals.title: Beyond the Pump: A Dynamic, Activity-Based Framework for Internalising the Full Social and Environmental Costs of Personal Travel
proposals.motivation: Despite policy goals for transport decarbonisation, prevailing pricing structures (e.g., fuel taxes, road tolls) fail to capture the heterogeneous external costs of travel, including carbon emissions, local air pollution, noise, and congestion. This mispricing distorts travel demand, undermines the business case for low-carbon modes like walking, and impedes the decoupling of mobility from emissions. A mechanism to accurately reflect these costs is critical for efficient and equitable climate policy in the transport sector.
proposals.hypothesis: A spatially and temporally differentiated pricing scheme, calibrated to the marginal external costs of different travel activities, will more effectively internalise social costs and induce a measurable shift towards lower-impact travel behaviours than static, vehicle-based taxation.
proposals.method: Building on stated-preference surveys and GPS/activity diary data, we will develop an agent-based simulation model of urban travel. The model will incorporate heterogeneous agents with different socio-demographic attributes and activity schedules. We will algorithmically apply a dynamic cost layer, where prices for car travel are calculated using a function of time-of-day, route-specific congestion (using real network data), vehicle-specific emissions factors, and localised air quality sensitivity. The model will be validated against observed travel data from a UK city and used to test the behavioural and emissions impacts of the proposed pricing against a baseline of current fuel duty.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a novel, empirically grounded framework for designing second-generation transport pricing policies that move beyond fuel taxation. It will contribute directly to the decoupling literature by quantifying the potential emissions reductions from cost-internalisation and offer practical insights for policymakers on the equity and acceptability of such schemes, linking fiscal and behavioural instruments.
proposals.novelty: While carbon pricing is well-studied, this approach integrates multiple externalities (carbon, local pollution, congestion) into a single, activity-based pricing mechanism at a high spatial-temporal resolution, directly connecting cost internalisation to daily activity patterns and testing it within an agent-based behavioural framework informed by Banister's prior work on feedback and energy use.

proposals.title: Decoupling Transport Externalities: A Comparative Life Cycle Assessment of Travel Modes for Urban Policy
proposals.motivation: Current transport policy and personal travel choices often rely on direct operational emissions (e.g., tailpipe CO2), neglecting the full environmental burden embedded in infrastructure, vehicle production, and fuel supply chains. This incomplete accounting risks misdirecting decarbonisation efforts and fails to capture the true external costs of different modes, undermining effective policy for achieving deep carbon reductions in line with Paris Agreement targets. A systematic, comparative LCA is needed to inform accurate carbon pricing, infrastructure investment, and behavioural interventions.
proposals.hypothesis: When a full life cycle assessment (LCA) boundary is applied, the ranking of travel modes by environmental external cost per passenger-kilometer will differ significantly from rankings based solely on direct operational energy use and emissions, with active travel modes (walking, cycling) demonstrating a decisive advantage that is currently under-valued in policy.
proposals.method: The research will apply a hybrid LCA framework, integrating process-based analysis for foreground systems (specific vehicle and infrastructure components) with environmentally extended input-output (EEIO) analysis for background systems (materials, energy supply). We will define functional units (e.g., passenger-km for a defined urban corridor) and system boundaries (cradle-to-grave) for key modes: private car (ICEV, BEV), bus, tram, cycling, and walking. Data will be drawn from established databases (e.g., Ecoinvent, GREET) and primary data collection on infrastructure lifespans and maintenance. The ReCiPe 2016 impact assessment method will be used to calculate midpoint (e.g., climate change, particulate matter) and endpoint (damage to human health, ecosystems) indicators, monetized using established external cost values (e.g., from the EU's Handbook on External Costs).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a robust, comparable dataset of monetized external costs across urban travel modes, directly applicable to cost-benefit analysis for transport projects. It will advance the methodological integration of LCA within transport studies, moving beyond simple energy analysis. The findings will offer critical evidence for policies aimed at genuine decoupling of mobility from environmental damage, supporting modal shift strategies and infrastructure prioritisation.
proposals.novelty: While LCA is established in product studies, its systematic application to compare the full external costs of all major urban passenger modes—including active travel—within a single, policy-oriented framework is novel. This integrates Banister's expertise in decoupling and behavioural feedback with rigorous environmental systems analysis, moving from measuring direct effects to quantifying systemic burdens.

proposals.title: Co-designing Decarbonised Mobility: A Participatory Framework for Aligning Citizen Practices with Transport Policy
proposals.motivation: Achieving deep decarbonisation in transport requires a fundamental shift in mobility culture, not just technological substitution. Top-down policies often fail due to public resistance or misalignment with daily life practices. Engaging citizens directly in designing both the policies and the cultural shift is critical for legitimacy and effectiveness, addressing the 'missing link' between individual behaviour and systemic carbon goals.
proposals.hypothesis: That a structured participatory design process, adapted from urban planning, will generate mobility policies and interventions that are more effective at reducing carbon emissions and increasing walking, as they are grounded in the lived experiences and co-created solutions of the citizen-participants.
proposals.method: The research will employ a multi-stage co-design methodology in a case study city. First, a stratified sample of households will participate in innovative feedback exercises on their electricity and travel data (building on Banister's prior work) to establish a baseline. Second, a series of participatory workshops will use design thinking tools (e.g., persona development, journey mapping, and backcasting) to collaboratively ideate policy packages and cultural interventions. Third, the co-designed proposals will be modelled using a disaggregated energy and travel demand framework to estimate potential carbon and modal shift impacts, comparing them to expert-led policy benchmarks.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, transferable framework for integrating participatory design into transport and carbon policy development. It will contribute empirical evidence on whether and how co-designed policies differ in substance and projected efficacy from traditional approaches, advancing the field of sustainable transport beyond technical-economic models towards socio-technical transition.
proposals.novelty: While participatory planning exists, its systematic application to forge a new, low-carbon *mobility culture*—specifically targeting the decoupling of travel from emissions—is novel. It uniquely combines Banister's expertise in feedback mechanisms and decoupling analysis with co-design methods to tackle the socio-behavioural core of the transport carbon problem.

proposals.title: Integrating Decarbonisation Policies: An Agent-Based Model of Urban Mobility as a Complex Adaptive System
proposals.motivation: Achieving deep decarbonisation in transport requires integrated policy packages, but their combined and emergent effects are poorly understood. Traditional transport models often fail to capture the complex feedbacks between infrastructure, pricing, social norms, and individual travel behaviour that determine policy success or failure. Modelling the urban mobility system as a complex adaptive system (CAS) is crucial for testing how policies interact to enable or hinder the decoupling of travel demand from carbon emissions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesise that policy integration (e.g., combining congestion charging, low-emission zones, and active travel investment) generates non-linear, emergent outcomes in carbon reduction and modal shift that are greater than the sum of individual policy effects, and that these outcomes are highly sensitive to heterogeneous agent behaviours and social networks.
proposals.method: We will develop an agent-based model (ABM) using a modular architecture in NetLogo or Python (Mesa). Agents (households/individuals) will be endowed with heterogeneous attributes (socio-demographics, preferences) and decision rules for modal choice, informed by discrete choice models and theories of planned behaviour. The environment will include a synthetic road/transit network, dynamic congestion, and policy levers. Key algorithms will include a path-finding module (A* or Dijkstra's) for route choice and a social influence module for norm diffusion. Calibration and validation will use pre-2026 data from UK travel surveys and energy feedback studies.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a novel computational laboratory for testing the systemic efficacy of integrated transport decarbonisation policies, moving beyond siloed assessments. It will contribute a new methodological framework for understanding mobility as a CAS, offering insights into the critical interactions between infrastructure, pricing, and behavioural change needed for deep carbon reduction.
proposals.novelty: While ABMs exist in transport, this proposal uniquely integrates Banister's decoupling and feedback research with CAS theory to explicitly model the emergent, non-linear outcomes of *policy integration* for decarbonisation, a significant advance over evaluating single policies in isolation.

proposals.title: Decoupling Transport Emissions through Metropolitan Parking Coordination: A Multi-Method Analysis of Energy and Behavioural Feedbacks
proposals.motivation: Achieving deep decarbonisation in transport requires systemic policy beyond city centres, as suburban car dependency undermines urban carbon goals. Pre-2026 research, including Banister's work on decoupling and feedback exercises, highlights the fragmented governance of parking as a key barrier. Coordinated metropolitan parking management is a critical but under-researched lever for reducing vehicle kilometres travelled (VKT) and associated emissions across functional urban regions.
proposals.hypothesis: That implementing a spatially and temporally differentiated, regionally coordinated parking pricing and supply policy will significantly reduce total metropolitan car travel energy use and carbon emissions, with effects amplified by positive feedback loops in travel behaviour compared to isolated city-centre interventions.
proposals.method: The research will employ a multi-method approach integrating geospatial analysis, agent-based modelling (ABM), and empirical case study evaluation. A metropolitan-scale ABM, calibrated with travel survey and parking inventory data, will simulate behavioural responses to policy scenarios (e.g., price gradients, permit areas). This will be coupled with an energy/emissions accounting framework to convert VKT changes. The model will be validated and supplemented with qualitative insights from stakeholder workshops in 2-3 case study regions, applying a feedback exercise methodology akin to Banister's prior household studies.
proposals.contribution: It will provide a novel, scalable framework for evaluating the carbon mitigation potential of integrated land-use and parking policy. The findings will advance the decoupling debate by quantifying the system-level energy and emissions impacts of coordinated versus fragmented parking management, offering evidence for polycentric governance.
proposals.novelty: It moves beyond the established focus on city-centre parking to model the metropolitan region as an integrated behavioural system, explicitly linking parking policy levers to regional energy and carbon outcomes through simulation and feedback mechanisms.

proposals.title: Neighbourhood Mobility Cultures: A Pilot Study on the Decoupling Potential of Integrated Land-Use and Mobility Interventions
proposals.motivation: Achieving deep decarbonisation in transport requires moving beyond technological substitution to address the systemic linkages between land use, energy demand, and travel behaviour. Macro-scale policies often fail to account for localised socio-technical contexts, or 'mobility cultures', which mediate the effectiveness of interventions. This gap is critical, as pre-2026 literature highlights the need for place-based strategies to enable absolute decoupling of travel demand from carbon emissions.
proposals.hypothesis: That implementing a coordinated package of physical (e.g., filtered permeability, mobility hubs) and social (e.g., personalised travel planning, resource feedback) interventions at the neighbourhood scale can catalyse a shift towards a low-carbon mobility culture, leading to measurable reductions in per capita vehicle kilometres travelled (VKT) and energy use.
proposals.method: The study will employ a longitudinal, quasi-experimental design comparing one intervention neighbourhood with a matched control. Data collection will integrate high-resolution GPS travel diaries, smart meter electricity readings, and repeated socio-psychological surveys. Analysis will use a Difference-in-Differences (DiD) framework to isolate the treatment effect, supplemented by structural equation modelling (SEM) to unpack the causal pathways between built environment changes, social feedback, attitudinal shifts, and final energy outcomes for transport and domestic use.
proposals.contribution: It will provide empirical evidence on the efficacy of a 'whole-system' neighbourhood pilot for carbon mitigation, directly informing scalable urban planning policy. The study will advance methodological integration by linking granular travel and energy data to theorise the co-evolution of infrastructure, practice, and culture.
proposals.novelty: While feedback and land-use studies exist in isolation, this research uniquely combines them in a real-world, place-based experiment to test their synergistic effect on creating a self-reinforcing low-carbon mobility culture, moving from measuring discrete behaviours to systemic change.

proposals.title: Decoupling Accessibility from Mobility: A Multi-Scale Framework for Land Use and Transport Planning to Reduce Travel Demand and Carbon Emissions
proposals.motivation: Achieving deep decarbonisation in transport requires moving beyond efficiency gains in vehicles to a structural reduction in travel demand. Pre-2026 policy and research often conflate mobility (movement) with accessibility (reaching destinations), leading to carbon-intensive planning. This research directly addresses the need to decouple well-being from high-energy mobility, a core challenge for sustainable transport.
proposals.hypothesis: Strategic, multi-scale optimisation of land-use configurations and transport network design can significantly reduce per capita vehicle kilometres travelled (VKT) while maintaining or improving aggregate levels of accessibility to essential services and opportunities.
proposals.method: The research will employ a multi-method approach. First, a spatial interaction model will quantify current accessibility for different demographic groups across selected UK city-regions. Second, building on Banister's previous feedback exercise methods, an agent-based model (ABM) will be developed to simulate household location and travel choices under different land-use scenarios (e.g., 15-minute city principles, transit-oriented development). The ABM will integrate discrete choice modelling for residential location and mode choice, with constraints on travel time budgets and energy use. Scenarios will be evaluated against metrics of VKT, energy use, carbon emissions, and equity of access.
proposals.contribution: It will provide a quantifiable, scenario-based framework for planners to evaluate land-use/transport interventions for demand reduction. It will advance the theoretical and empirical understanding of 'decoupling' by measuring the elasticity of travel demand to integrated accessibility planning, contributing directly to low-carbon transport policy.
proposals.novelty: While accessibility concepts are established, this proposal's novelty lies in the integrated, multi-scale modelling that explicitly links fine-grained land-use optimisation with behavioural feedback mechanisms (inspired by Banister's prior work) to test the systemic carbon reduction potential of prioritising accessibility over mobility.

proposals.title: Simulating Decoupling Pathways: An Agent-Based Model for Assessing Urban Transport Policy Bundles on Energy Use and Carbon Emissions
proposals.motivation: Achieving absolute decoupling of transport activity from carbon emissions is a critical urban policy challenge. While macro-level analyses and stated preference studies exist, there is a gap in understanding how combinations of pricing, infrastructure, and information policies interact to influence the complex, adaptive decisions of individual travelers at a city scale. This limits the ability to predict emergent system outcomes, such as total emissions and modal shifts, with confidence.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that policy bundles integrating fiscal, physical, and informational interventions will produce synergistic, non-linear reductions in transport energy use and carbon emissions that exceed the sum of their individual effects, primarily by triggering a cascade of behavioral adaptations across heterogeneous traveler agents.
proposals.method: We will develop a spatially explicit agent-based model (ABM) using a modified MATSim architecture, calibrated with empirical travel survey and energy use data from a UK city. Agents will be endowed with heterogeneous socio-demographic attributes and utility functions for mode choice (including walking), incorporating explicit energy and cost sensitivity parameters informed by Banister's prior feedback exercise research. Policy scenarios (e.g., congestion charging, low-emission zones, walking infrastructure investment, personalized travel planning) will be implemented as modular rules affecting agent perceptions and constraints. The model will run Monte Carlo simulations to analyze emergent distributions of travel demand, energy consumption, and CO2 emissions.
proposals.contribution: This research will contribute a novel, empirically-grounded simulation tool for ex-ante assessment of urban transport policy mixes. It will advance the theoretical understanding of decoupling by quantifying behavioral synergies and rebound effects at a micro-scale. The findings will provide actionable insights for policymakers on designing effective, equitable policy bundles to meet carbon reduction targets.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in integrating Banister's core themes—decoupling, energy, and detailed travel behavior—into a dynamic ABM framework to test policy bundles, moving beyond static or single-policy evaluations. It uniquely combines fine-grained behavioral feedback mechanisms with system-wide energy and emission accounting.

proposals.title: Quantifying Decoupling Potential: A Micro-Scale Energy and Emissions Assessment of the 15-Minute Neighborhood Concept
proposals.motivation: Achieving deep decarbonization in transport requires a fundamental shift from efficiency gains in vehicles to absolute reductions in travel demand, particularly from fossil-fueled cars. The '15-minute city' concept, gaining traction pre-2026, posits that hyper-local accessibility can facilitate this shift, but robust, quantitative evidence linking neighborhood design to measurable changes in energy use and carbon emissions is sparse. This research directly addresses the 'decoupling' challenge by testing if spatial reorganization can sever the link between essential activity fulfillment and high-carbon travel.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that neighborhoods designed as self-sufficient '15-minute units' will demonstrate a statistically significant decoupling of resident well-being from motorized travel distance, leading to measurably lower per capita transport energy consumption and CO2 emissions compared to conventional suburban layouts, even when controlling for socio-economic factors.
proposals.method: The research will employ a comparative case-study design, integrating GIS-based spatial analysis with empirical energy and travel behavior data. We will first develop a 'Self-sufficiency Potential Index' (SPI) algorithm using network analysis on OpenStreetMap data to quantify access to amenities (e.g., groceries, healthcare, education) within a 15-minute walk/cycle isochrone for sampled neighborhoods. This SPI will then be correlated with data on household travel behavior (from travel diaries) and derived transport energy use (applying pre-2026 energy intensity factors per mode from sources like the UK's National Travel Survey). The analysis will use multivariate regression to isolate the effect of spatial design from other variables, drawing on Banister's prior work on feedback mechanisms in travel behavior.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a novel, quantitative framework for assessing the carbon mitigation potential of hyper-local urban planning, moving the discourse from conceptual advocacy to evidence-based policy. It will contribute a validated methodology for measuring 'decoupling' at the micro-scale and offer concrete insights into which amenity mixes most effectively reduce car-dependent travel, directly informing low-carbon spatial policy.
proposals.novelty: While the 15-minute concept is discussed in urban studies, this proposal's novelty lies in its specific application of Banister's 'decoupling' and energy-focused transport research framework to the neighborhood scale, and its technical integration of spatial network algorithms with granular behavioral energy data to test the concept's core environmental premise.

proposals.title: Modelling the Long-Term Urban Form Response to Energy Price Shocks and Carbon Policies: An Agent-Based Simulation for Decoupling Transport Emissions
proposals.motivation: Urban and transport planning has long focused on how spatial form determines energy use and emissions, a static view of causality. However, achieving deep decarbonisation requires understanding the dynamic, long-term feedback where escalating energy costs and stringent carbon policies could fundamentally reshape urban structure, land use, and travel demand. This reverse causality is critical for robust, adaptive policy but remains underexplored in empirical modelling, risking lock-in or maladaptive infrastructure investments.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesise that sustained high energy prices and targeted carbon taxation will induce non-linear shifts in urban form over a 30-year horizon, accelerating densification and increasing the modal share of walking and cycling, but that these effects are significantly mediated by policy design and existing urban morphology.
proposals.method: We will develop an agent-based model (ABM) integrating pre-2026 frameworks (e.g., UrbanSim, MATSim) to simulate household and firm location choices, transport mode selection, and developer behaviour under different energy-cost scenarios. The core technical innovation is the bidirectional feedback loop: energy costs influence location and travel choices (via a utility maximisation algorithm), which in turn alter land values and development patterns, iteratively reshaping the urban form that then redefines future travel energy use. Scenarios will test carbon taxes, clean technology subsidies, and their spatial targeting, calibrated using historical data from Swedish and Norwegian cities.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a dynamic, policy-sensitive simulation tool to forecast the co-evolution of urban form and transport energy demand, moving beyond static impact assessments. It will deliver novel insights into the conditions under which energy policies can catalyse a structural decoupling of mobility from carbon emissions, directly informing integrated spatial and climate policy.
proposals.novelty: The proposal inverts the classic 'form-determines-flow' causality by explicitly modelling how energy flows (costs) recursively reshape form, a dynamic feedback largely absent from contemporary urban energy models. It uniquely integrates Banister's expertise in decoupling, behavioural feedback, and policy evaluation within a single, forward-looking simulation architecture.

proposals.title: Dynamic Decoupling: Modelling the Co-evolution of Telework, Urban Mobility, and Residential Energy Demand
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026, the rapid, post-pandemic shift towards hybrid and remote work has created a structural break in travel and energy demand patterns, challenging static models of transport and emissions. This evolution interacts with changing social norms around mobility and urban form, creating a feedback loop that existing decoupling studies, which assume fixed socio-economic structures, fail to capture. Understanding this dynamic is critical for predicting long-term carbon trajectories and designing effective urban and energy policy.
proposals.hypothesis: The decoupling of transport energy use from economic activity is non-linear and path-dependent, driven by the co-evolution of telework adoption, induced changes in non-work travel and residential location, and the resultant reconfiguration of total household energy demand (transport + residential).
proposals.method: We will develop a system dynamics model integrating pre-2026 UK time-use and expenditure survey data with smart meter and travel diary data. The model architecture will feature interconnected feedback loops between: 1) a telework adoption module (using diffusion of innovation theory), 2) a travel behaviour module (estimating changes in trip frequency, mode shift—especially walking—and induced long-distance leisure travel), and 3) a residential energy module. Calibration and validation will use longitudinal panel data from pre-2026 studies like the 'Innovative Feedback Exercise' to test the model's predictive power against observed changes.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a novel, dynamic framework for assessing the net carbon impact of telework, moving beyond simplistic 'commute savings' calculations. It will offer policymakers a simulation tool to anticipate unintended consequences (e.g., energy demand rebound) and identify synergistic interventions in urban planning and digital infrastructure to lock in decarbonisation gains.
proposals.novelty: It explicitly models the two-way, dynamic interaction between a changing social practice (telework) and its spatial/energy consequences over time, integrating transport and residential energy systems in a way that Banister's prior feedback exercise began to explore but did not formalise into a predictive model.

proposals.title: Decoupling Urban Transport from Carbon Emissions: A Comparative Framework Analysis Across Diverse City Contexts
proposals.motivation: Achieving deep decarbonisation in transport requires understanding how urban form, policy, and culture interact to enable or inhibit decoupling travel demand from emissions. Pre-2026 literature, including Banister's own work, often focuses on European or high-income case studies, limiting the generalisability of frameworks for decoupling. Expanding analysis to a more diverse set of cities is critical to identify context-sensitive pathways for global climate mitigation in the transport sector.
proposals.hypothesis: The efficacy of specific policy bundles (e.g., pricing, technology, infrastructure for walking) in decoupling transport activity from carbon emissions is not universal but is significantly moderated by a city's geographic, economic, and cultural context. We hypothesise that walking and transit-oriented decoupling is more robust across contexts than technology- or price-focused strategies alone.
proposals.method: We will apply and extend the multi-method framework from Banister's prior work on household feedback and policy analysis. For 12-15 cities selected across geographies and development levels, we will conduct a structured comparative case study analysis. This involves: 1) Calculating city-level transport energy and carbon intensity metrics using available datasets (e.g., UITP, OECD); 2) Deploying a policy coding schema to categorise interventions (tax, technology, infrastructure); 3) Using statistical methods (e.g., panel regression, Qualitative Comparative Analysis) to test for associations between policy mixes, urban form variables (density, mix), and decoupling trends over a 15-year period.
proposals.contribution: This research will produce a typology of cities based on their decoupling trajectories and effective policy levers, moving beyond one-size-fits-all recommendations. It will directly contribute to the IPCC and urban policy discourse by providing empirically grounded, context-specific evidence on transport decarbonisation. The expanded framework will be a tool for other researchers to benchmark cities.
proposals.novelty: While comparative urban transport studies exist, this proposal uniquely integrates the decoupling paradigm with a systematic analysis of policy effectiveness across a deliberately diverse sample, testing the transferability of frameworks developed primarily in European contexts. It combines macro-level energy/emissions data with meso-level policy analysis in a novel comparative design.

proposals.title: Decoupling at the Doorstep: A Composite Energy Analysis of Neighborhood-Scale Transport and Building Interactions for Low-Carbon Planning
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 climate policy emphasizes sectoral decarbonization, but the critical interactions between building energy use and personal travel demand at the local scale are poorly quantified. This gap hinders effective local planning, as interventions in one domain (e.g., building retrofits) may inadvertently increase emissions in another (e.g., induced car travel). A granular, composite energy analysis is needed to inform integrated urban planning that genuinely reduces total carbon footprints.
proposals.hypothesis: Neighborhoods with similar aggregate demographics but differing built-form characteristics (e.g., density, mix of uses, walkability) will exhibit statistically significant differences in their composite per-capita energy use for transport and domestic purposes, revealing key leverage points for local carbon mitigation.
proposals.method: The study will apply and extend composite energy accounting methods from Banister's prior work to the neighborhood scale. For a sample of 30-50 neighborhoods in a UK city, we will construct a composite energy metric integrating: 1) direct household electricity/gas use from smart meter or sub-national statistics, 2) embodied energy of private vehicle use derived from travel diary surveys and vehicle stock data, and 3) energy for public transport using operator data and passenger volumes. Statistical analysis (multiple regression, cluster analysis) will model the relationship between this composite metric and built-environment variables (density, land-use mix, walkability scores, public transport accessibility), controlling for socio-economic factors.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a novel, operationalizable framework for quantifying the total energy footprint of neighborhoods, moving beyond sectoral silos. It will deliver evidence-based metrics to help planners evaluate the net carbon consequences of local development decisions, directly supporting the integration of transport and energy policy at the spatial scale where they intersect.
proposals.novelty: While composite energy analysis exists at national or household levels, its application to the neighborhood as a functional planning unit is novel. It uniquely combines fine-grained travel behavior feedback methods with built-form data to test decoupling hypotheses at a spatially relevant scale for intervention.

proposals.title: Constrained Growth: Modelling Transport Futures with Economic Output as the Primary Objective
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 climate policy and transport research often treats economic growth as a constraint or an externality, framing decarbonisation as a cost. This can lead to politically untenable pathways and underplay the potential for innovation within a growth-oriented system. A model that explicitly treats economic output as the imperative, subject to hard constraints on social equity and environmental limits, would better reflect the political reality of policy-making and reveal the necessary conditions for a just transition.
proposals.hypothesis: By inverting the standard modelling paradigm, we hypothesise that pathways maximising economic output within defined carbon, energy, and equity constraints will identify different, and potentially more politically feasible, transport policy portfolios (e.g., technology mandates, spatial planning, pricing) than those derived from cost-minimisation or emissions-target models.
proposals.method: We will develop a novel system dynamics model, integrating pre-2026 transport-energy-economy (TEE) frameworks. The core algorithm will be a constrained optimisation routine (e.g., using a genetic algorithm or linear programming solver) that maximises a composite economic output indicator (e.g., GDP, accessibility-weighted economic activity) for a defined region. Hard constraints will be applied: a declining carbon budget (aligned with 1.5°C pathways), a minimum standard of transport equity (measured via the Gini index of accessibility), and a total energy use cap. The model will be calibrated using Swedish and Norwegian data, building on Banister's prior comparative work on policy effectiveness.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a new analytical lens for transport policy, directly linking economic performance to sustainability and equity outcomes. It will generate concrete, ranked policy portfolios that satisfy multiple objectives under a growth imperative, offering pragmatic insights for policymakers. The work will extend decoupling theory by testing its limits and prerequisites within an inverted modelling framework.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the fundamental inversion of the objective function in transport-energy-economy modelling, treating the economy as the variable to maximise rather than a cost to minimise. This formalises a politically salient but academically underexplored perspective, moving beyond cost-benefit analysis to a constrained optimisation of prosperity.

proposals.title: Operationalising the Three Imperatives: Developing and Validating Transport System Indicators for Human Needs, Social Equity, and Environmental Limits
proposals.motivation: Transport policy and research, particularly around decoupling travel from carbon emissions, often focuses narrowly on environmental or technological efficiency. This risks neglecting fundamental human needs (e.g., access) and social equity, potentially leading to unsustainable or unjust outcomes. Grounded in pre-2026 sustainable transport discourse, this research addresses the critical gap in translating the conceptual 'three imperatives' (human needs, social equity, environmental limits) into a coherent, measurable framework for systemic evaluation.
proposals.hypothesis: A composite set of empirically measurable indicators, integrating data on transport energy use, travel behaviour, and socio-demographics, can reliably capture the performance of a transport system against the three imperatives, revealing critical trade-offs and synergies.
proposals.method: The method builds on Banister's recent work on feedback mechanisms and decoupling analysis. We will first conduct a systematic literature review to identify candidate indicators (e.g., 'accessibility to key services' for human needs, 'Gini coefficient of transport energy use' for equity, 'carbon intensity per passenger-km' for environmental limits). Using Swedish and Norwegian national travel survey and energy data (pre-2026), we will apply principal component analysis and correlation matrices to test for independence and coherence. Validation will involve applying the indicator set to contrasting urban and rural case studies, assessing their sensitivity to policy levers like pricing or infrastructure for walking.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first validated, operational indicator set for the three imperatives in transport, moving from theory to measurement. It will offer a practical tool for policymakers to assess the multi-dimensional sustainability of transport interventions. The research will also advance methodological integration in transport studies by explicitly linking energy, travel behaviour, and equity metrics.
proposals.novelty: While individual indicators exist, the novelty lies in their systematic integration into a single framework specifically designed to measure the tensions and compatibilities between the three imperatives, using robust statistical validation on real-world travel and energy data.

proposals.title: Operationalizing the Three-Imperative Model: A Policy Tool for Decoupling Transport Emissions from Regional Development
proposals.motivation: Achieving deep decarbonization in transport requires moving beyond technological fixes to integrate social and behavioral imperatives with environmental and economic goals. Existing policy models often fail to systematically link these dimensions, leading to fragmented strategies that struggle to achieve absolute decoupling of emissions from development. Grounding this in pre-2026 knowledge, the urgent need for integrated planning tools is evident from the Paris Agreement targets and the persistent growth in transport emissions in many developed economies.
proposals.hypothesis: The three-imperative model (environmental, social, economic) can be operationalized into a quantitative policy assessment framework that reveals specific, synergistic policy packages for a given region, which are more effective at achieving absolute decoupling than single-instrument approaches.
proposals.method: The research will adapt and extend the three-imperative conceptual model into a system dynamics simulation for a specific national/regional case (e.g., a UK region). It will integrate pre-2026 datasets on transport energy use, travel behavior (modal split, trip rates), and socio-economic indicators. The core algorithm will model feedbacks between policy levers (e.g., carbon tax, investment in active travel infrastructure, telework promotion) and system outcomes across the three imperatives, using scenario analysis to test policy bundles. The method builds directly on Banister's prior work on feedback in household energy and travel behavior.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, practical tool for policymakers to design and stress-test integrated sustainable transport strategies. It will contribute empirical evidence on the quantified synergies and trade-offs between environmental, social, and economic goals in a real-world planning context, advancing the theoretical discourse on decoupling.
proposals.novelty: While the three-imperative model is established conceptually, its translation into a dynamic, quantitative simulation model for specific policy planning is novel. This moves the model from a heuristic framework to a testable policy instrument, explicitly linking behavioral feedbacks (walking, travel choice) with macro-level emissions and economic outcomes.

proposals.title: Operationalising Decoupling: A Scalable Imperative Model for Urban Carbon and Energy Footprint Assessment
proposals.motivation: Cities are critical arenas for achieving climate mitigation, yet local planners often lack robust, integrated frameworks to measure and manage the decoupling of urban well-being from transport and energy-related carbon emissions. Existing top-down national indicators fail to capture the unique mobility patterns, built form, and behavioural feedbacks at the community scale, hindering effective local policy. This gap is acute for active travel (walking), a key low-carbon mode whose systemic benefits are often undervalued in strategic planning.
proposals.hypothesis: That a city-scale adaptation of the 'imperative model' of decoupling, integrating granular data on travel behaviour and energy use, can provide a valid and actionable diagnostic tool for urban sustainability, revealing specific leverage points where infrastructure and policy can reduce carbon intensity while maintaining accessibility.
proposals.method: The research will adapt the imperative model's analytical structure to the urban scale by defining system boundaries for a case-study city. It will employ a mixed-methods approach: 1) developing a set of composite indicators for transport carbon intensity (using distance-based methods), residential energy intensity, and active travel accessibility; 2) applying spatial analysis and GIS mapping to link these indicators to neighbourhood typologies; and 3) conducting a scenario analysis using a simplified decomposition method (e.g., ASIF) to test the impact of different policy bundles (e.g., low-emission zones, walking infrastructure investment) on the decoupling trajectory.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a transferable methodological framework for city-level decoupling assessment, directly contributing to the practice of urban sustainability planning. It will advance theoretical understanding of how feedbacks between travel behaviour, built environment, and energy use manifest at a local scale, offering evidence to prioritise investments in walking and low-carbon mobility.
proposals.novelty: While decoupling is studied nationally, this proposal novelly downscales the imperative model to an operational urban planning tool, explicitly integrating active travel metrics with energy and carbon indicators from the transport and residential sectors to create a holistic community diagnostic.

proposals.title: Socio-spatial inequalities in the carbon and health co-benefits of active travel: A scenario-based analysis for UK cities
proposals.motivation: Transport decarbonisation and public health policies often assume uniform adoption of active travel (walking, cycling) across populations. However, pre-2026 evidence shows travel behaviour is strongly shaped by socio-economic and built environment factors. Ignoring this heterogeneity risks overestimating aggregate carbon savings and health benefits, while obscuring distributional inequities, thereby undermining effective and just policy design.
proposals.hypothesis: The carbon and health co-benefits of active travel promotion are significantly overestimated in scenarios assuming uniform adoption, and the distribution of these benefits will be spatially and demographically unequal, favouring higher-income groups in well-serviced neighbourhoods.
proposals.method: We will develop an agent-based model (ABM) using NetLogo or a similar platform, integrating pre-2026 UK travel survey data (e.g., National Travel Survey) and geodemographic classifications (e.g., ACORN). Agents will be parameterised by age, income, and neighbourhood type. Scenarios will test uniform vs. heterogeneous adoption rules, where adoption probability is a function of agent attributes and local walkability indices. The model will simulate modal shift, using pre-2026 energy/emission factors (e.g., DEFRA conversion factors) and health assessment methods (e.g., WHO HEAT) to quantify differential carbon and health outcomes.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a novel, quantified assessment of how socio-spatial inequalities modulate the aggregate and distributional outcomes of active travel policies. It will advance the decoupling debate by demonstrating that social context is a critical mediator between behavioural change and system-level carbon reduction, offering a more nuanced framework for policy appraisal.
proposals.novelty: While Banister's prior work has identified feedback in behaviour and measured policy effects, this proposal uniquely integrates socio-spatial heterogeneity directly into scenario modelling of co-benefits, moving beyond average population estimates to expose the equity dimensions of transport decarbonisation.

proposals.title: Beyond the Inevitable: Stress-Testing 2030 Transport Projections with Alternative Baseline Scenarios for Robust Decarbonisation Policy
proposals.motivation: Current business-as-usual (BAU) transport projections for 2030, used to inform climate policy, are often linear extrapolations of past trends and may be critically fragile. They risk underestimating the potential for rapid, non-linear shifts in technology adoption, energy prices, or economic structures, leading to flawed policy assessments of mitigation measures. This research addresses a core gap in transport decarbonisation planning by systematically challenging the stability of these foundational BAU assumptions.
proposals.hypothesis: Incorporating plausible but unexpected technological and economic shifts into alternative baseline scenarios will reveal that official 2030 BAU transport projections are not a robust 'neutral' reference, but are instead highly sensitive to specific assumptions, thereby altering the measured effectiveness of proposed carbon mitigation policies.
proposals.method: The research will construct a set of alternative 2030 baseline scenarios for a case study region (e.g., UK or Scandinavia), integrating pre-2026 insights on feedback mechanisms from energy and travel behaviour. Using a scenario analysis framework, we will model shifts such as accelerated EV cost parity, sustained high fossil fuel price volatility, or a step-change in telework adoption post-pandemic. The analysis will employ decomposition techniques to quantify the impact of each shift on transport energy demand and emissions, contrasting results with the official BAU projection to assess policy effectiveness under different futures.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a methodological framework for creating more robust and stress-tested transport baselines, moving beyond single-point forecasts. It will directly contribute to the literature on decoupling transport growth from emissions by demonstrating how the baseline itself influences the perceived 'gap' needing to be filled by policy, offering critical insights for policymakers on the risks of over-reliance on conventional projections.
proposals.novelty: While scenario analysis is common for *policy* pathways, this proposal uniquely applies it to stress-test the *baseline* projection itself, explicitly linking unexpected socio-technical shifts to the evaluation framework for transport carbon policies, an approach underdeveloped in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Equity in the Decoupling Dividend: A Distributional Analysis of Health Co-benefits from Low-Carbon Transport Scenarios
proposals.motivation: Research on transport decarbonization often highlights aggregate health co-benefits from increased active travel and reduced air pollution. However, pre-2026 evidence suggests these benefits are not equitably distributed, potentially reinforcing existing socioeconomic disparities. This study directly addresses this gap by inverting the primary analytical focus from aggregate health gains to their distributional equity, a critical consideration for just transition policies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that low-carbon transport scenarios, while delivering net positive health outcomes, will disproportionately concentrate benefits (e.g., from improved walkability and air quality) in already advantaged groups, while displacing risks and costs (e.g., from fuel poverty or reduced accessibility) onto more deprived populations.
proposals.method: The research will employ a scenario-based, quantitative distributional analysis. Building on Banister's prior work on decoupling and feedback mechanisms, we will develop distinct UK transport policy scenarios (e.g., stringent carbon tax, clean technology subsidy, active travel investment). For each scenario, we will use spatial microsimulation to link transport energy and travel behaviour data (e.g., National Travel Survey) with health determinants and socioeconomic indices (e.g., Index of Multiple Deprivation). The core analysis will apply concentration indices and Lorenz curves to quantify the distribution of estimated health outcomes (e.g., disability-adjusted life years saved) across income and deprivation quintiles.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, evidence-based framework for evaluating the equity dimensions of transport decarbonization, moving beyond aggregate cost-benefit analysis. It will offer policymakers explicit metrics to identify and mitigate regressive outcomes in climate action, thereby contributing to more socially sustainable decoupling strategies.
proposals.novelty: While health co-benefits are well-studied, systematically modeling their socioeconomic distribution as the primary outcome of transport scenarios is novel. It integrates decoupling analysis with established methods from health equity research, applying them to forward-looking policy scenarios rather than historical data.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Health Co-benefits and Trade-offs of Transport Decarbonisation: An Integrated Model of Physical Activity Gains and Road Traffic Injury Risks
proposals.motivation: Policies promoting active travel (walking, cycling) are central to decarbonising transport and improving public health through increased physical activity. However, pre-2026 models often treat these health outcomes—physical activity benefits and road traffic injury risks—separately or simplistically, potentially over- or under-estimating the net health impact of modal shift scenarios. This gap hinders evidence-based policy that maximises co-benefits while minimising unintended harms.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating high-resolution exposure-response functions for physical activity and injury risk into a single transport-energy-emissions scenario framework will reveal non-linear trade-offs and synergies, demonstrating that the net health outcome of decarbonisation policies is highly sensitive to local infrastructure and safety interventions.
proposals.method: We will extend an existing energy-emissions scenario model (e.g., from Banister's work on decoupling) with an agent-based microsimulation module. This module will use algorithms to assign trip chains and modes to synthetic populations, drawing on travel survey data. Physical activity gains (in MET-hours) will be calculated using the WHO HEAT methodology, while injury risks will be modelled using spatial interaction models that incorporate road type, traffic speed, and separation infrastructure as key parameters. Scenario testing will compare 'technology-focused' vs. 'behaviour-focused' decarbonisation pathways.
proposals.contribution: This research will produce a novel, integrated assessment tool for policymakers to evaluate the combined health impacts of transport scenarios. It will provide empirical evidence on the conditions under which decarbonisation delivers net health gains, contributing directly to the 'co-benefits' literature in climate and health policy. The work will advance methods for coupling behavioural, energy, and health outcome modelling.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the concurrent, spatially-disaggregated modelling of two critical but opposing health outcomes within a single transport decarbonisation framework, moving beyond aggregate estimates to expose localised trade-offs. This integrates Banister's expertise in decoupling and feedback mechanisms with granular health impact assessment.

proposals.title: Decoupling Urban Mobility: A Comparative Policy Analysis for Accelerating Active Travel Uptake
proposals.motivation: Achieving deep decarbonization in transport requires a systemic shift away from car dependency, with active travel (walking, cycling) offering a critical pathway for short urban trips.
