However, policy interventions to promote active travel are often implemented in isolation, with limited understanding of their comparative effectiveness and synergistic potential in enhancing perceived safety and public acceptability. Grounded in pre-2026 decoupling and behaviour change literature, this research addresses a key gap in translating policy ambition into measurable, on-the-ground modal shift.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrated policy packages combining physical infrastructure investment (e.g., protected cycle lanes, pedestrian zones) with targeted behavioural feedback and incentive mechanisms will yield significantly greater increases in active travel mode share and perceived safety than any single policy instrument applied in isolation.
proposals.method: The research will employ a mixed-methods, comparative case study design across 3-4 European cities with differing policy landscapes. Quantitative analysis will use a Difference-in-Differences (DiD) econometric framework on panel travel survey data to isolate the causal effect of specific policy introductions on active travel rates and carbon emissions. This will be complemented by qualitative analysis of policy documents and semi-structured interviews with planners and citizens, informed by Banister's prior work on feedback mechanisms, to assess perceptions of safety and acceptability. A synthesis will model the 'policy mix' effectiveness using multi-criteria decision analysis.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a robust, evidence-based ranking of policy effectiveness for active travel uptake, directly informing urban climate action plans. It will advance the theoretical understanding of decoupling by quantifying the carbon mitigation potential of non-motorized mobility policies and elucidating the socio-technical interactions between infrastructure, behaviour, and perception.
proposals.novelty: While existing research often evaluates single policies, this study's novel contribution is its systematic, comparative focus on *policy packages* and their interaction effects, explicitly linking infrastructure measures to behavioural feedback insights—a synthesis underexplored in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Decoupling Transport's Dual Burden: An Integrated Assessment of Carbon Emissions, Traffic Noise, and Mental Well-being in Urban Mobility Systems
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research on transport decoupling has focused primarily on energy and carbon emissions, with health assessments often limited to physical activity and air pollution. Traffic noise is a pervasive urban stressor, with established pre-2026 evidence linking it to annoyance, sleep disturbance, and adverse mental health outcomes, yet it remains a missing component in holistic transport policy evaluation. Integrating noise and mental well-being into decoupling analysis is critical for assessing the true co-benefits of sustainable mobility transitions, such as shifts to walking.
proposals.hypothesis: That pathways for decoupling transport growth from carbon emissions which also reduce traffic noise exposure (e.g., modal shift to walking, electrification) will yield significant, quantifiable co-benefits for population mental well-being, and that these benefits are currently undervalued in policy appraisal.
proposals.method: The study will extend the analytical frameworks from Banister's prior work on household feedback and carbon mitigation. We will employ a quasi-experimental, longitudinal panel design, combining GIS-based noise modelling (using CNOSSOS-EU or similar pre-2026 standards) with validated psychometric scales for mental well-being (e.g., Warwick-Edinburgh Mental Well-being Scale) and travel diaries. Statistical analysis will use structural equation modelling (SEM) to disentangle the direct and mediated pathways between transport exposure variables (noise levels, travel mode choice), carbon footprints, and mental well-being outcomes, controlling for socio-economic confounders.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a novel, integrated metric for assessing the 'total health dividend' of transport decarbonisation, moving beyond a singular carbon focus. It will deliver empirical evidence on the mental health co-benefits of noise-reducing mobility policies, directly informing cost-benefit analyses and sustainable urban planning. The work will advance methodological integration in transport studies by linking physical environmental modelling with behavioural and psychological data.
proposals.novelty: While noise and mental health are studied in environmental epidemiology, and decoupling in transport studies, their integrated quantitative assessment within a transport decoupling and policy evaluation framework is novel. The explicit coupling of noise exposure models with travel behaviour data and well-being outcomes to evaluate decarbonisation strategies represents a significant non-obvious extension of Banister's existing work on feedback and carbon mitigation.

proposals.title: Modelling the Health Co-Benefits of Decoupling: An Agent-Based Simulation of Travel Mode Choice and Physical Activity Outcomes
proposals.motivation: Transport decarbonisation strategies often focus on technological substitution, neglecting the significant health co-benefits from active travel. While the decoupling of transport activity from carbon emissions is a key policy goal, the parallel decoupling of travel from sedentary lifestyles remains under-explored. This gap limits the ability to quantify the full societal value of policies promoting walking and cycling, a core theme in Banister's work on sustainable mobility.
proposals.hypothesis: Agent-based models that integrate empirically-grounded social and psychological feedback mechanisms—similar to those explored in household energy and travel behaviour studies—can reveal how transport policies non-linearly influence aggregate population health through emergent changes in individual travel mode choices and physical activity levels.
proposals.method: We will develop an agent-based model (ABM) using a framework like NetLogo or MATSim, where agents represent heterogeneous individuals with attributes (e.g., socio-demographics, attitudes) and decision rules for mode choice. The core architecture will integrate a utility-based discrete choice model, informed by pre-2026 travel behaviour literature, with dynamic feedback loops that adjust agent preferences based on social norms and perceived health benefits, akin to the innovative feedback exercise methodology. Health outcomes will be modelled by translating active travel minutes into metabolic equivalent (MET) scores and estimating population-level changes in disease burden.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a novel simulation tool to quantify the health co-benefits of transport carbon mitigation policies, moving beyond static cost-benefit analysis. It will contribute to the decoupling literature by explicitly modelling the dual decoupling of travel from emissions and from ill-health, offering policymakers a more holistic evidence base for promoting active travel.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in applying a dynamic, feedback-rich ABM from social sciences—a method not yet mainstream in transport decarbonisation studies—specifically to simulate emergent health outcomes from travel choices, thereby bridging the gap between transport energy analysis and public health modelling in a single computational framework.

proposals.title: Decoupling Travel Demand: A Discrete Choice Model for Predicting Mode Shifts Towards Walking and Low-Carbon Transport
proposals.motivation: Achieving deep decarbonization in transport requires understanding and predicting behavioral shifts away from private car use. Pre-2026 policy evaluations, such as those on carbon taxes in Scandinavia, often lack granular behavioral foundations, while feedback studies on household energy and travel show complex, non-linear responses. A robust, theoretically grounded method is needed to forecast how policy mixes can induce a sustained modal shift to walking and active travel, which is critical for decoupling mobility from emissions.
proposals.hypothesis: That a nested logit discrete choice model, incorporating latent variables for environmental attitudes and habitual constraints, will significantly improve the predictive accuracy of long-term mode shift probabilities in response to combined carbon pricing and active travel infrastructure policies.
proposals.method: The research will employ a stated preference survey design to collect data on mode choices under varied policy scenarios (e.g., carbon tax levels, walking infrastructure quality). We will specify a hybrid choice model (HCM), integrating a discrete choice core (Multinomial and Nested Logit) with latent variable models (using Structural Equation Modelling) for attitudes and habits. Estimation will be performed using maximum simulated likelihood in software such as Apollo for R or Biogeme, allowing for the explicit modelling of correlation in unobserved factors among sustainable modes.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated, transferable modelling framework that quantifies the interaction between hard economic instruments and soft behavioral factors in travel mode choice. It will directly contribute to the decoupling literature by offering a method to more accurately forecast the emissions reduction potential of policy packages aimed at promoting walking.
proposals.novelty: While discrete choice models are standard in transport, their integration with latent psychological constructs—specifically tailored to the context of carbon mitigation and active travel—within a policy evaluation framework for decoupling represents a novel synthesis of Banister's core themes.

proposals.title: Decoupling Urban Futures: A Land-Use Transport Interaction (LUTI) Model to Quantify the Carbon and Energy Implications of Walking-Oriented Development
proposals.motivation: Achieving deep decarbonisation in transport requires moving beyond incremental vehicle efficiency gains to address the systemic relationship between urban form and travel demand. Pre-2026 research, including Banister's work on decoupling, highlights the need to integrate long-term land-use feedbacks into transport-energy-emissions modelling. This is critical for evaluating whether policies promoting active travel, like walking, can durably reduce carbon emissions or if induced demand and spatial reorganisation erode these gains.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating a dynamic, agent-based LUTI model with a high-resolution energy and emissions accounting framework will reveal that walking-oriented urban development policies, while effective in the short term, trigger significant long-term land-use changes that partially offset carbon savings unless coupled with stringent complementary constraints on car-oriented development and energy supply.
proposals.method: The research will develop and calibrate an open-source LUTI model for a UK case-study city, extending frameworks like UrbanSim or SILO. It will integrate: 1) an agent-based demographic and location choice module, 2) a multimodal transport assignment module with explicit walking network impedance, and 3) a granular energy/emissions module derived from Banister's previous work on household travel behaviour. The model will run policy scenarios (e.g., pedestrianisation, parking restrictions, densification) over a 30-year horizon, using difference-in-difference simulation analysis to isolate the causal contribution of land-use feedbacks to travel energy use and carbon emissions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, empirically grounded methodology to quantify the 'true' long-term carbon effect of walking-oriented transport policies, accounting for urban system feedbacks. It will advance the decoupling debate by moving from static correlations to dynamic, causal simulation, offering policymakers a tool to design more robust policy packages that lock in carbon savings.
proposals.novelty: While LUTI models exist, their integration with detailed, behaviourally-informed energy and carbon accounting specific to active travel policies is novel. The approach uniquely focuses on the temporal mismatch between short-term travel behaviour changes and long-term urban structural change, a critical gap in transport decarbonisation planning identified in Banister's prior feedback exercise research.

proposals.title: Decoupling Transport Emissions from Health Impacts: A Lifecycle Assessment of Active and Motorised Travel Modes
proposals.motivation: Current transport decarbonisation policies often focus narrowly on tailpipe emissions, potentially overlooking significant health and environmental burdens from vehicle manufacturing and energy production. This limited system boundary risks misinforming policy, as the promotion of electric vehicles or certain biofuels may shift, rather than reduce, negative health impacts. Integrating lifecycle assessment (LCA) from industrial ecology into transport analysis is therefore critical for a holistic understanding of true decoupling.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesise that expanding the system boundary to include lifecycle stages will significantly alter the ranking of transport modes by total health impact, revealing that the benefits of walking are substantially greater, and the net benefits of some 'low-carbon' motorised modes are smaller, than conventional tailpipe-only analyses suggest.
proposals.method: The research will apply a hybrid LCA framework, combining process-based analysis for vehicle manufacturing (using pre-2026 databases like Ecoinvent) and an input-output approach for broader economic inputs. For a defined urban travel context (e.g., London), we will model the lifecycle health impacts (in DALYs) of passenger-km for walking, cycling, conventional cars, and electric vehicles. The analysis will integrate established impact assessment methods (e.g., ReCiPe) for emissions from fuel production, electricity generation, and material processing, directly linking them to health endpoints.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous, health-integrated LCA model tailored for urban transport policy evaluation, moving beyond simple carbon metrics. It will deliver empirical evidence on the full health co-benefits of active travel and identify potential burden-shifting risks in the transition to electric mobility, offering a more robust evidence base for sustainable transport planning.
proposals.novelty: While LCA is common in industrial ecology, its integration into Banister's decoupling and behavioural travel research is novel, creating a new synthesis of macro-level environmental accounting with micro-level travel behaviour insights. The explicit focus on health impacts, rather than just carbon, across the full lifecycle of both active and motorised modes represents a significant advance in transport studies.

proposals.title: Decoupling at the Micro-Scale: A Corridor-Level Assessment of Transport Interventions for Carbon and Energy Reduction
proposals.motivation: While national and city-level decoupling of transport activity from carbon emissions is a key policy goal, the efficacy of specific local interventions remains poorly quantified. Pre-2026 literature highlights a gap in applying rigorous, integrated assessment frameworks to the neighborhood or corridor scale, where walking and short-trip modal shift potential is highest. This research addresses the 'missing link' between macro-level policy and measurable, localized behavioral and environmental outcomes.
proposals.hypothesis: That a corridor-level application of an integrated energy-carbon assessment framework will reveal a non-linear relationship between specific physical interventions (e.g., pedestrianization, traffic calming) and carbon emissions, mediated strongly by changes in short-distance travel behavior and energy use patterns.
proposals.method: The study will adapt the assessment framework from Banister's prior work on household electricity and travel feedback, applying it to a single urban corridor. It will employ a quasi-experimental, before-and-after design with control. Data collection will combine smart meter electricity data, GPS-enabled travel diaries, and traffic counts. Analysis will use a difference-in-differences model to isolate the intervention effect, coupled with structural equation modelling to trace the pathways from physical change, through behavioral adaptation (mode choice, trip frequency), to final energy and carbon outcomes.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated, micro-scale methodology for ex-post evaluation of transport interventions, moving beyond theoretical models. It will generate concrete evidence on which specific measures most effectively decouple local mobility from carbon emissions, directly informing targeted, cost-effective urban planning. The findings will bridge the gap between high-level decoupling discourse and practical, neighborhood-level implementation.
proposals.novelty: It uniquely transposes a household-level feedback framework to a spatially bounded corridor system, integrating real-time energy and travel micro-data to dissect the causal chain of intervention impacts. This granular, systemic evaluation at the 'missing middle' scale of urban analysis is novel within pre-2026 decoupling research.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Health Co-benefits of Decarbonising National Transport Networks: A Systems Model for Inter-City Travel and Freight
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research on transport decarbonisation has focused heavily on urban areas and passenger cars, with health co-benefits (e.g., from reduced air pollution and increased physical activity) assessed at the city level. National and regional transport networks, encompassing inter-city travel and freight, constitute a major and growing source of emissions, yet their potential health co-benefits from systemic shifts (e.g., modal shift to rail, logistics efficiency) remain poorly quantified and excluded from policy appraisal. This gap limits a holistic understanding of the true societal value of deep decarbonisation strategies for the transport sector.
proposals.hypothesis: That a systemic shift towards low-carbon modes in national/regional passenger and freight networks will generate significant, quantifiable health co-benefits, and that these benefits will vary non-linearly depending on the specific technological and behavioural pathways chosen for decarbonisation.
proposals.method: We will extend an existing energy-GHG model (e.g., akin to the UK Transport Carbon Model or the Swedish/Norwegian frameworks from Banister's prior work) to incorporate health impact assessment modules. The method involves: 1) Developing scenario-based projections for national transport demand (passenger-km, tonne-km) to 2050, defining 'Business as Usual', 'Technology-Focused', and 'Behavioural Change' pathways. 2) Using life-cycle assessment and direct emission factors to calculate spatially disaggregated changes in air pollutants (PM2.5, NOx). 3) Applying established dose-response functions (e.g., from the WHO or integrated assessment models like GAINS) to quantify avoided mortality and morbidity. 4) Implementing a comparative feedback analysis to contrast the health outcomes of technology-push versus behaviour-pull policies.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide the first integrated, scalable model to estimate the health co-benefits of decarbonising national-scale passenger and freight transport systems. It will deliver a critical evidence base for transport and health policymakers, demonstrating the added value of co-benefits in cost-benefit analyses of major infrastructure and regulatory decisions. It will also advance methodological integration between transport energy modelling and health impact assessment.
proposals.novelty: While health co-benefits are studied for urban transport, applying this lens to the complex, multi-modal system of national/regional networks—particularly including freight—is novel. The explicit comparison of health outcomes from technological versus behavioural decarbonisation pathways directly extends Banister's recent work on measuring 'true effects' and household feedback into the systemic, macro-scale domain.

proposals.title: Modelling the Long-Term Health Co-Benefits of Transport Decarbonisation: A Dynamic, Path-Dependent Approach to Walking Uptake and Infrastructure Investment
proposals.motivation: Achieving deep decarbonisation in transport requires a systemic shift to active travel, which promises significant public health co-benefits. However, existing impact assessments are largely static, failing to capture how gradual behavioural change and long-term infrastructure investment interact over time. This limits the ability of policymakers to design and sequence interventions that maximize cumulative health gains while reducing emissions.
proposals.hypothesis: The long-term health co-benefits of transport decarbonisation policies are significantly underestimated by static models, and are contingent on the specific temporal sequencing (path dependency) of behavioural nudges and infrastructure investment that support walking.
proposals.method: We will develop a dynamic, agent-based simulation model (ABM) grounded in energy and travel survey data. The ABM will incorporate heterogeneous household agents whose travel mode choices evolve via a feedback mechanism, informed by Banister's prior work, where past walking behaviour influences future propensity to walk. Infrastructure investment (e.g., pedestrian networks) will be introduced as a time-variable constraint, altering the choice environment. We will use system dynamics modules to simulate the co-evolution of behaviour and infrastructure over a 20-year horizon, employing Monte Carlo simulations to test different policy sequences and assess path dependency.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a novel, temporally explicit framework for quantifying the cumulative health impacts of transport policy pathways. It will offer evidence on the optimal sequencing of 'soft' behavioural and 'hard' infrastructure measures to accelerate decoupling. The findings will directly inform strategic, long-term urban and transport planning for health and carbon mitigation.
proposals.novelty: It integrates the co-benefits and feedback dynamics of behavioural change with the time-lagged effects of capital investment in a single simulation framework, moving beyond cross-sectional or simple forecast models to explicitly model path dependency in transport-health systems.

proposals.title: Network Resilience and Decoupling: An Ecological Network Analysis of Bulgaria's Transport Corridors
proposals.motivation: Achieving deep decarbonization of transport requires understanding the resilience of transport systems to shocks and their capacity to support modal shift. Bulgaria's strategic position in the EU's TEN-T network, combined with its reliance on carbon-intensive road freight, makes it a critical case for analyzing how corridor structure influences emissions and vulnerability. This pre-2026 context demands new analytical tools to move beyond simple volume metrics and assess systemic properties.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying ecological network analysis (ENA) to Bulgaria's transport corridors will reveal that resilience is not uniformly distributed, and that critical structural weak points (e.g., specific nodes or links) are major barriers to achieving decoupling of transport activity from carbon emissions.
proposals.method: We will model Bulgaria's primary road, rail, and inland waterway corridors as a directed, weighted network, with nodes representing major hubs and links representing freight/passenger flows (tonne-km, passenger-km). Using pre-2026 ENA algorithms, we will compute system-level indices (e.g., Ascendency, Development Capacity) and node/link-level metrics (e.g., Keystoneness, Node Pair Degree) to quantify resilience and identify critical components. The analysis will be conducted for different scenarios, including disruptions and simulated modal shifts towards rail and active travel (walking), to test structural responses.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, systems-based framework for assessing transport corridor resilience, directly linking network topology to decarbonization potential. It will offer Bulgarian and EU policymakers a spatially explicit tool to identify priority investments for hardening infrastructure and facilitating low-carbon modal shift, contributing to the 'decoupling' literature with a structural, network-based perspective.
proposals.novelty: While ENA is established in ecology, its application to transport corridors for resilience and decoupling analysis is novel. This approach moves beyond traditional economic or engineering models by treating transport flows as a metabolic network, revealing emergent properties and interdependencies not captured by link-by-link analysis.

proposals.title: Strategic Interactions for Decarbonisation: A Game-Theoretic Model of Public-Private Investment in Low-Carbon Transport Infrastructure
proposals.motivation: Achieving deep decarbonisation in transport requires massive investment in low-carbon infrastructure (e.g., EV charging, active travel networks). This investment is a shared responsibility between governments and private investors, yet their strategic interactions—often misaligned in objectives and risk—are poorly understood, leading to policy inefficiency and investment shortfalls. Grounded in Banister's work on decoupling and behavioural feedback, this research addresses the critical gap in modelling these strategic dynamics to accelerate the transition.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesise that cooperative game solutions (e.g., the Shapley value) can identify fair and stable cost/revenue-sharing mechanisms for low-carbon transport projects, while non-cooperative sequential games will reveal how specific policy instruments (taxes vs. subsidies) can strategically steer private investment towards socially optimal outcomes.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage game-theoretic model. First, a non-cooperative, sequential game with perfect information will model a government (leader) setting a policy (carbon tax, technology subsidy) and private investors (followers) making investment decisions. Second, a cooperative coalitional game will model joint public-private project financing, using the Core and Shapley value to analyse stability and fair cost allocation. The model will be parameterised using pre-2026 energy and travel data (e.g., from Banister's Swedish/Norwegian tax-tech study) and solved via backward induction and linear programming for coalitional solutions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formal analytical framework to diagnose and design more effective public-private partnerships for transport decarbonisation. It will contribute to the decoupling literature by explicitly modelling the strategic behaviour underlying investment, offering policymakers a tool to assess the efficacy of fiscal instruments versus cooperative agreements in mobilising private capital.
proposals.novelty: While game theory is applied in energy economics, its integration into transport decarbonisation policy analysis, specifically to model the strategic interplay of fiscal policy and private investment risk in infrastructure, is novel. It extends Banister's comparative policy work by providing a dynamic strategic model, not just an ex-post evaluation.

proposals.title: Backcasting Transport Futures: Deriving Infrastructure and Behavioural Needs from Decarbonisation and Well-being Targets
proposals.motivation: Traditional transport planning starts with projected demand, often leading to infrastructure that locks in high-carbon mobility and fails to meet broader societal goals. With urgent climate targets (e.g., Paris Agreement) and growing recognition of the health and social benefits of active travel, a paradigm shift is needed. This research addresses this by inverting the planning process, starting explicitly with development goals to define permissible transport systems.
proposals.hypothesis: A backcasting methodology, which defines specific, quantified development targets (e.g., per capita carbon budgets, modal share for walking, accessibility indices) and works backwards, will identify a fundamentally different and more sustainable portfolio of necessary transport investments and behavioural interventions than conventional forecasting.
proposals.method: The research will employ a mixed-methods backcasting framework. First, it will establish a multi-criteria goal set (e.g., 80% emissions reduction by 2040, 30% of trips by walking). Second, it will use system dynamics modelling to explore the energy and travel behaviour feedbacks necessary to achieve these goals, building on Banister's prior work. Third, it will apply scenario analysis and spatial accessibility modelling (using GIS-based algorithms) to derive the specific infrastructure configurations (e.g., pedestrian network density, public transport connectivity) and policy packages required, treating the goals as fixed constraints.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a practical, goal-oriented planning tool for policymakers, moving beyond abstract decoupling concepts. It will contribute a formalised 'inverse' methodology to the transport studies literature and generate concrete, evidence-based pathways showing how transport systems must evolve to serve non-transport objectives like health, equity, and climate stability.
proposals.novelty: While backcasting exists in sustainability science, its rigorous application to derive *specific* transport needs from *integrated* development goals (combining carbon, health, and economic access) is novel. It fundamentally reverses the causal logic of standard transport models, making goals the independent variable and infrastructure the dependent variable.

proposals.title: Generalizing Decoupling through Behaviour Change: A Multi-City Evaluation of Personalized Travel Plans for Carbon Mitigation in Medium-Sized European Cities
proposals.motivation: Achieving transport decarbonization requires shifting travel behaviour away from car dependence, particularly in medium-sized cities where infrastructure and modal options differ from large metropolises. While Personalized Travel Plans (PTPs) show promise, evidence of their effectiveness is often based on single-city case studies (e.g., Burgos), limiting understanding of transferability and scalability. This research addresses a critical gap in the decoupling literature by systematically testing whether PTPs can reliably reduce emissions across diverse urban contexts.
proposals.hypothesis: The carbon mitigation efficacy of a standardized PTP intervention is context-dependent; its success in medium-sized cities will be moderated by pre-existing modal share, public transport provision, and the strength of the feedback provided, rather than being uniformly effective.
proposals.method: The study will employ a quasi-experimental, longitudinal design, implementing a standardized PTP intervention (involving travel diaries, tailored advice, and feedback) in 4-6 medium-sized European cities with varying transport systems. Pre- and post-intervention data on travel behaviour (mode, distance) and associated energy/emissions will be collected via surveys and activity-based modelling. A difference-in-differences analysis will compare treatment and control groups, while multivariate regression will identify key moderating variables (e.g., city-level infrastructure, household characteristics) influencing outcomes.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide robust, generalizable evidence on the potential of soft transport measures to contribute to carbon decoupling in a critical but understudied city size category. It will produce a transferable evaluation framework and identify the contextual conditions necessary for PTPs to succeed, informing more effective and targeted local climate policy.
proposals.novelty: Moving beyond single-case validation, this research explicitly tests the transferability hypothesis of behavioural interventions, integrating Banister's decoupling and feedback concepts into a comparative, multi-city experimental design rarely applied to medium-sized urban areas.

proposals.title: Synergistic Decoupling: Quantifying the Combined Effect of Personalised Travel Planning and Cycling Infrastructure on Carbon Emissions
proposals.motivation: Achieving deep decarbonisation in urban transport requires a shift from private motorised travel to active modes like cycling. While the separate impacts of 'soft' Personalised Travel Planning (PTP) measures and 'hard' cycling infrastructure are studied, their combined, potentially synergistic effect on modal shift and carbon emissions remains poorly quantified. This gap hinders effective, integrated policy design for sustainable mobility and transport-energy decoupling.
proposals.hypothesis: The combination of targeted PTP interventions with new, high-quality cycling infrastructure will produce a greater-than-additive (synergistic) shift towards cycling and reduction in transport-related carbon emissions, compared to either measure implemented in isolation.
proposals.method: The study will employ a longitudinal, quasi-experimental design in a UK city implementing new cycling infrastructure. Households within the intervention corridor will be randomly assigned to a control group, a PTP-only group, and a combined PTP+infrastructure group. Pre- and post-intervention travel behaviour data will be collected via GPS-enabled smartphone apps and validated travel diaries. The analysis will use a difference-in-differences framework, coupled with structural equation modelling, to isolate the interaction effect between the soft and hard measures on cycling mode share, vehicle kilometres travelled, and derived carbon emissions using pre-2026 DEFRA/UK government conversion factors.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide robust, empirical evidence on the synergy between 'soft' behavioural and 'hard' infrastructural transport policies, directly informing integrated urban planning. It will advance the theoretical understanding of decoupling by quantifying how combined interventions accelerate the dissociation of travel demand from carbon emissions, offering a replicable methodological framework for policy evaluation.
proposals.novelty: While Banister's prior feedback exercise work isolated behavioural responses, this study innovatively integrates that behavioural lens with a physical infrastructure intervention, testing for non-linear interaction effects—a critical but overlooked dimension in transport decarbonisation research pre-2026.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Health and Climate Adaptation Co-benefits of Public Transport Policy-Induced Active Mobility
proposals.motivation: Public transport policies (PTP) are primarily evaluated for their direct emissions reductions, yet their role in inducing active mobility (walking, cycling) creates significant ancillary benefits. Pre-2026 research, including Banister's work on decoupling and feedback exercises, highlights a critical gap: the lack of integrated, quantified evidence linking PTP to specific health outcomes and urban climate adaptation (e.g., reduced heat island effect, improved flood resilience). This quantification is essential for robust, multi-criteria policy appraisal and for building public and political support for sustainable transport transitions.
proposals.hypothesis: Public transport policies that increase active mobility generate measurable, significant co-benefits in population health (e.g., reduced morbidity from non-communicable diseases) and urban climate adaptation capacity, which, when monetized, constitute a substantial portion of the total policy benefit.
proposals.method: The research will employ a quasi-experimental, longitudinal panel design in a UK city implementing a major PTP (e.g., a congestion charge expansion or bus network overhaul). We will combine GPS/accelerometer data from a participant panel (n=500) with detailed travel diaries and health surveys to measure changes in active travel duration and intensity. Health benefits will be quantified using established dose-response functions (e.g., WHO's Health Economic Assessment Tool) for physical activity, air pollution exposure, and road traffic risk. Adaptation benefits will be modelled by linking reduced private vehicle use to lower urban heat island intensity (using a simplified urban canopy model) and reduced surface runoff (using a land-use change and impervious surface model). A comparative cost-benefit analysis will integrate these monetized co-benefits with direct carbon mitigation estimates.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, integrated framework for quantifying the full spectrum of PTP co-benefits, moving beyond carbon-centric evaluations. It will deliver empirical, monetized estimates of health and adaptation gains, directly informing transport and urban planning policy. The study will also advance methodological integration of transport, health, and climate adaptation modelling.
proposals.novelty: While co-benefits are acknowledged conceptually, this study's novelty lies in its simultaneous, empirical quantification of both health and hard-to-measure climate adaptation outcomes specifically triggered by PTP, using a integrated panel-data and spatial modelling approach that links individual behavioural data to systemic urban environmental changes.

proposals.title: Mapping Social Contagion: A Network Analysis of Peer-to-Peer Diffusion for Walking and Low-Carbon Travel
proposals.motivation: Achieving deep decarbonisation in transport requires a significant shift in daily travel behaviour, moving beyond technological substitution. While feedback interventions show promise, their reach is limited. Understanding and leveraging the naturally occurring social networks through which behaviours and norms spread offers a powerful, under-explored pathway to accelerate the adoption of sustainable modes like walking.
proposals.hypothesis: Sustainable travel behaviours, specifically increased walking and reduced car use, diffuse through social networks via mechanisms of social influence and normative pressure, and targeting central or bridging individuals within these networks can significantly amplify the rate and scale of behavioural adoption.
proposals.method: The research will employ a mixed-methods social network analysis (SNA) framework. First, ego-network surveys and name-generator questionnaires will map the social ties (advice, friendship, co-travel) within a defined community (e.g., a residential development). Second, longitudinal travel diaries and GPS/self-report data will track changes in walking and car use. Using stochastic actor-oriented models (SAOMs) in RSIena, we will statistically test for network effects on behavioural co-evolution, controlling for homophily. Key network metrics (e.g., betweenness centrality, eigenvector centrality) will identify potential diffusion agents.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first empirical, network-based model of how low-carbon travel behaviours diffuse socially, moving beyond individualistic behavioural models. It will generate practical insights for designing 'social diffusion' interventions that leverage peer networks, offering a complementary strategy to traditional policy instruments for achieving transport decoupling.
proposals.novelty: While SNA has been applied to health behaviours, its application to the diffusion of specific, measurable low-carbon travel behaviours (like walking) is novel. It directly builds on Banister's prior work on feedback and decoupling by shifting the unit of analysis from the household to the socially embedded individual within a network structure.

proposals.title: Decoupling Business Mobility: A Novel Feedback and Optimization Method for Freight and Service Vehicle Emissions
proposals.motivation: Urban freight and service mobility is a critical, yet under-researched, component of transport emissions, with last-mile delivery and service calls contributing significantly to urban carbon footprints and congestion. Pre-2026 literature shows a strong focus on passenger travel behaviour, but a lack of scalable, evidence-based methods to help businesses understand and reduce their commercial vehicle energy use. This gap hinders progress in decoupling economic activity from transport emissions.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying a tailored, data-driven feedback and optimization method, adapted from proven Personal Travel Planning (PTP) approaches, to business freight and service operations will identify significant, actionable opportunities for reducing vehicle kilometers traveled (VKT) and associated carbon emissions without compromising service quality.
proposals.method: The method adapts the PTP consultancy framework into a three-stage Business Mobility Planning (BMP) tool. First, we will develop a data collection protocol using GPS loggers and digital duty sheets to establish a detailed baseline of service/freight vehicle movements, stops, and idle times for a cohort of small-to-medium enterprises. Second, an algorithm will analyze this data to cluster trips, identify inefficiencies (e.g., circuitous routing, high empty running), and model alternative scenarios (e.g., route bundling, mode shift for light parcels). Third, a structured feedback exercise, inspired by Banister's prior work on household electricity, will present these personalized insights and optimization options to business managers, measuring their intent and capacity to change.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a novel, scalable methodology for measuring and mitigating emissions from the business mobility sector, directly extending decoupling research into a neglected domain. It will produce empirical evidence on the energy and carbon reduction potential of operational changes in last-mile logistics, offering policymakers and businesses a practical tool beyond technological substitution.
proposals.novelty: While PTP is well-established for passengers, its systematic adaptation to the operational decision-making of businesses, particularly for service vehicles and small-scale freight, is novel. The integration of granular vehicle telematics with behavioral feedback mechanisms tailored to commercial, rather than personal, travel motivations represents a significant advance.

proposals.title: Beyond Movement: A Configurational Analysis of Urban Network Correlations with Social and Economic Outcomes for Decarbonisation
proposals.motivation: Urban transport research, particularly in decoupling mobility from carbon emissions, has predominantly focused on movement flows, neglecting how the static spatial configuration of streets itself correlates with key social and economic variables. Understanding these non-movement correlations is crucial for designing urban forms that inherently support low-carbon lifestyles (e.g., walking) and equitable access, which are central to deep decarbonisation goals. This gap limits the ability of planners to proactively shape places that reduce travel demand and its associated energy use.
proposals.hypothesis: The configurational properties of urban street networks, measured via space syntax techniques (e.g., integration, choice, connectivity), exhibit statistically significant correlations with key socio-economic data (e.g., deprivation indices, local business vitality, residential density) that are independent of, or predictive of, observed movement patterns. These correlations can reveal latent structural opportunities or barriers for low-carbon travel behaviour.
proposals.method: The research will employ a configurational approach using established space syntax software (DepthmapX) to analyse axial and segment maps of a selected UK city region. Network measures (integration RN, choice, metric mean depth) will be calculated at multiple radii. These will be spatially correlated, using Geographic Information Systems (GIS) and statistical modelling (e.g., spatial regression, Spearman's rank correlation), with granular pre-2026 datasets: the Index of Multiple Deprivation, business rates data, and dwelling density. The analysis will control for standard transport variables to isolate the effect of network configuration.
proposals.contribution: This work will extend the theoretical application of configurational analysis beyond pedestrian/vehicular movement modelling to directly interface with socio-economic planning objectives for sustainability. It will provide empirical evidence on how urban form fundamentally correlates with social and economic outcomes, offering a novel diagnostic tool for evaluating urban plans against decarbonisation and equity goals. It directly contributes to Banister's work on decoupling by linking the 'hard' infrastructure of streets to the 'soft' variables of behaviour and opportunity.
proposals.novelty: While space syntax is established for modelling movement, its systematic application to correlate with broad socio-economic datasets (rather than just movement counts) for decarbonisation policy is novel. It integrates configurational analysis with the energy and behavioural feedback perspectives from Banister's recent work, creating a new synthesis between urban morphology and travel demand management.

ideas.title: From Watersheds to Streetsheds: Applying Hydrological Basin Analysis to Urban Configurational Space
ideas.description: This research seed challenges the fundamental assumption in urban configurational analysis (e.g., Space Syntax) that streets are best represented as axial or segment lines. Drawing an analogy from hydrology, where the analysis shifted from river-center lines to volumetric watersheds using DEMs, we propose a similar paradigm shift for urban analysis. The analogous solution involved using topographic elevation data to define catchment areas based on gravitational flow. We adapt this by conceptualizing urban space as a volumetric permeability field, where factors like building geometry, topography, and programmatic openings define a 'configurational topography.' Using this 3D model, we can algorithmically delineate 'streetsheds'—the volumetric public space area whose simplest, most direct visual or pedestrian paths lead to a given street. This moves the unit of analysis from the line of the street itself to the full area of urban space it configures and serves, enabling a more nuanced understanding of urban integration, centrality, and movement potential.

ideas.title: Urban movement as a network flow problem: Prioritizing connectivity and resistance over Euclidean distance
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that metric distance is the primary navigational criterion in urban movement models. Drawing an analogy from Circuit Theory, where current flow is determined by network topology and path resistance rather than wire length, the solution is to model urban movement similarly. The urban network (streets, paths) is conceptualized as a graph where edges are assigned a 'movement resistance' weight. This resistance is a function of geometric factors (e.g., number of intersections, angular deviation, visual permeability) and topological factors (e.g., node degree, hierarchical status, integration core). Pedestrian or vehicular 'flow' is then modeled as seeking paths of least resistance, using adapted network flow algorithms. This fundamentally reorients urban movement prediction from a distance-minimization problem to a topology-and-form-driven impedance minimization problem.

ideas.title: Inverse Network Design for Psychological Risk Mitigation in Urban Mobility
ideas.description: This research direction applies the inverse problem framework from network science to urban mental health epidemiology. It seeks to compute optimal street network configurations (e.g., connectivity, route directness, exposure to stressors) that would induce movement flows minimizing not just post-traumatic stress symptoms (PTSS), but also the risk of comorbid conditions like depression and anxiety disorders arising from daily mobility. This moves beyond merely analyzing existing networks for correlation, to actively designing future-proof infrastructure that disrupts the pathways to multiple adverse psychological sequelae. The core novelty is the formal optimization of physical urban form against a multi-dimensional psychological risk model derived from mobility data.

ideas.title: Applying ecological foraging theory experiments to validate urban configurational measures
ideas.description: This research adapts experimental paradigms from behavioral ecology's foraging theory to validate urban configurational measures. In behavioral ecology, controlled foraging experiments test animal movement efficiency in artificial landscapes with manipulated resource distributions and connectivity. The adapted approach creates controlled urban navigation tasks where participants locate resources in real or simulated grids while configurational variables (integration, connectivity, visibility) are systematically manipulated. By measuring human navigation efficiency, decision patterns, and cognitive load across different spatial configurations, this method provides direct experimental validation of which configurational measures best predict human wayfinding performance in urban environments.

ideas.title: Network-Structure Stress: Validating Aggregate Mobility Models with Physiological Ground Truth
ideas.description: This research direction posits that if aggregate urban flows were purely a function of network topology (Idea A), then an individual's physiological stress markers during navigation should be uncorrelated with specific path choices predicted by the model. By instrumenting participants with wearable sensors to capture objective stress data (extending Idea B beyond self-report), we can test this null hypothesis. A significant correlation between model-predicted high-flow routes and elevated physiological stress would falsify the pure network model, quantifying the 'cognitive load' or 'subjective cost' layer missing from structural analyses. Thus, the synergy lies in using physiological ground truth not merely to supplement questionnaires, but to directly stress-test and calibrate abstract mathematical models of urban mobility, bridging macro-scale network science with micro-scale affective neuroscience.

ideas.title: Psychophysiological Urban Navigation: Biomarker Correlates of Cognitive Load and Stress in Real-World Wayfinding
ideas.description: This research direction integrates psychoneuroimmunology biomarkers with cognitive science experimental paradigms to study urban navigation. Participants would perform controlled wayfinding tasks in real urban grids while wearable sensors collect real-time physiological data (e.g., heart rate variability, electrodermal activity) and pre/post-task samples assay inflammatory markers (e.g., IL-6) and cortisol. This allows researchers to correlate moments of high cognitive load, disorientation, or navigational success/failure with immediate autonomic nervous system activity and subsequent biological dysregulation. The resulting data provides a direct, multi-system biological validation for configurational measures of urban design, moving beyond behavioral performance to reveal the hidden physiological impact of city layouts on human well-being.

ideas.title: Mechanistic Multi-Layer Framework for Spatial-Social Network Interdependency
ideas.description: This research moves beyond simple correlation to model the specific causal mechanisms by which the configurational properties of a spatial movement network (e.g., a street network) influence the topology and function of an embedded non-movement network (e.g., innovation collaboration, economic transaction). It formalizes testable hypotheses about mechanisms like brokeraged access, exposure, and infrastructural coupling. The methodology employs a multi-layer network model with explicit inter-layer dependency links, analyzed using causal inference and network control techniques to robustly quantify the effect of spatial configuration while accounting for confounding structural factors.

ideas.title: Urban Trauma Topographies: Idiographic Wayfinding Studies of Extreme PTSS/PTG in Real City Grids
ideas.description: This research applies controlled cognitive science wayfinding paradigms to individuals exhibiting extreme post-traumatic stress symptoms (PTSS) or post-traumatic growth (PTG) scores, conducting intensive single-case studies within real urban grids. By tracking navigation paths, errors, and physiological/self-report measures during wayfinding tasks, it quantifies how trauma-altered cognitive and affective states (e.g., hypervigilance, avoidance, or renewed purpose) interact with objective configurational measures like integration and connectivity. This idiographic, experimental approach moves beyond aggregate correlations to model the precise mechanisms by which personal psychological history dynamically filters spatial perception, offering a new lens on urban experience and potentially informing trauma-informed urban design.

ideas.title: Topological Resilience in Regional Transportation Networks: Identifying Configurations that Promote System Recovery
ideas.description: This research inverts the typical flow-prediction paradigm by applying topological configurational measures (e.g., betweenness, closeness, integration) to large-scale road or rail networks to identify structural features that correlate with systemic resilience, rather than just vulnerability. It assumes that most components of a network are fundamentally resilient and seeks to understand how their topological arrangement collectively enables the entire system to withstand and recover from major disruptions like natural disasters or targeted attacks. The core methodology involves analyzing historical disruption and recovery data alongside network topology to build models that distinguish between configurations that merely sustain high flows and those that actively promote recovery. The outcome is a set of evidence-based, topological design principles for creating or retrofitting regional transportation infrastructure that is inherently resilient.

ideas.title: Applying Protein Folding Energy Landscapes to Architectural Navigation Complexity
ideas.description: This research adapts the protein folding energy landscape framework from computational biophysics to the domain of indoor navigation and building layouts. Instead of modeling atomic interactions and free energy, we construct a 'cognitive energy landscape' for an architectural network, where each spatial location or decision node is assigned a cognitive cost value based on configurational metrics (e.g., isovist area, axial connectivity, line-of-sight to landmarks). Navigation is then simulated as a stochastic process on this landscape, akin to a folding protein searching for its native state. Key destinations act as deep basins of attraction. By applying transition state theory and analyzing the funnel-like properties of the landscape, we can predict probable pedestrian routes, identify high-cognitive-cost transition zones (confusion areas), and propose layout modifications to smooth the landscape, thereby enhancing intuitive wayfinding. This method moves beyond static graph analysis to a dynamic, probabilistic model of navigation as a path-finding process on a cognitively-weighted configurational surface.

ideas.title: Spatial-Configurational Network Analysis of Post-Trauma Experience
ideas.description: This research direction synthesizes configurational analysis of urban space with psychometric network modeling to study post-trauma outcomes. It moves beyond line-based street networks to model an individual's accessible environment as a continuous volumetric field—a 'configurational life-space'—defined by permeability, visibility, and spatial integration. A multilayer network model then integrates this spatial configuration layer with a psychometric network layer of PTSS, PTG, and support items, allowing analysis of how specific spatial properties (e.g., isolation, connectivity to support nodes) dynamically influence the activation, strength, and feedback loops within the psychological symptom network. This enables a truly ecological and person-centered analysis of how built and social environments are constitutive elements of mental health trajectories after trauma.

ideas.title: Dynamic Affordance-Aware Cognitive Modeling for Real-Time Indoor Navigation
ideas.description: This research develops a hybrid cognitive-configurational model for indoor navigation that overcomes the limitation of static architectural networks by integrating real-time dynamic affordances. The system fuses a static spatial network derived from building layouts with a dynamic perceptual network constructed from IoT sensor data and computer vision, identifying transient landmarks, crowd flow, and operational states. Cognitive inference algorithms then operate on this time-augmented network model to predict wayfinding difficulty, optimize signage placement, and provide adaptive navigation instructions. This approach enables accurate modeling of navigation in complex, evolving interiors like hospitals or transportation hubs where the environment is never purely architectural.

ideas.title: Apply causal inference for distributional impacts
ideas.description: Use econometric and machine learning methods to estimate policy impacts on different subpopulations for profile construction.
ideas.sourceTitle: Distributive justice and equity in transportation

ideas.title: Scale down to indoor navigation and building layouts

ideas.title: Inverse problem: identify pathways from thresholds

ideas.sourceTitle: The Imperatives of Sustainable Development

ideas.title: Refine indicator thresholds and data sources
ideas.description: Improve the precision and robustness of the four-dimensional assessment framework's indicators and underlying data.
ideas.sourceTitle: Sustainable development: Our Common Future revisited

ideas.title: Urban Flow Inference for Population-Level Trauma Trajectory Mapping
ideas.description: This research develops and validates a method to infer longitudinal trajectories of post-traumatic stress symptoms (PTSS) and post-traumatic growth (PTG) at a community or population level by applying cognitive inference techniques to longitudinal, aggregate urban flow data. By correlating known trauma events (e.g., natural disasters, community violence) with shifts in patterns of mobility, social congregation, and routine activity extracted from anonymized urban datasets, the research aims to model how collective psychological adaptation unfolds over years. This provides a macro-scale complement to traditional cohort studies, enabling the tracking of recovery and resilience in entire cities or affected subgroups where direct psychological assessment is impractical.

ideas.title: Scale down to project-level equity assessment
ideas.description: Apply a simplified PPPA framework to evaluate the distributive justice of a single infrastructure project.
ideas.sourceTitle: Distributive justice and equity in transportation

ideas.title: Modeling Survivorship Trajectories: A Network-Based Approach to Psychological Outcomes Across Cancer Types
ideas.description: This research synthesizes the comparison of psychological outcomes in cancer survivors with spatial network modeling principles. Instead of merely comparing mean scores between hematological and solid tumor survivors, it conceptualizes the multidimensional space of psychological outcomes (e.g., PTSD, anxiety, depression, post-traumatic growth) as a network or landscape. The core analysis investigates whether the 'topological structure' of this psychological landscape—such as the connectivity between different symptom clusters, the presence of distinct 'basins of attraction,' or the pathways to resilience—differs fundamentally between cancer type groups, analogous to how urban movement is shaped by geometry rather than simple distance. This reveals not just if outcomes differ, but how the entire architecture of the survivorship experience varies.

ideas.title: Question the universality of the three imperatives
ideas.description: Examine if different cultural contexts define needs, equity, or limits differently.
ideas.sourceTitle: The Imperatives of Sustainable Development

ideas.title: Causal Isolation of Topological Effects on Flows in Regional Networks
ideas.description: This research moves beyond correlation to establish the causal impact of a network's topological configuration on transportation flows. It employs a causal inference framework, using instrumental variables (like historical network plans) or quasi-experimental designs from network disruptions to isolate topology's effect from powerful confounders like population and economic activity. The system first models expected flows based purely on exogenous demand factors, then quantifies the marginal improvement in prediction accuracy attributable to configurational measures. This approach yields robust, actionable insights for network planners by identifying which topological features genuinely shape flow efficiency.

ideas.title: Apply portfolio theory to transport investment risk
ideas.description: Treat different transport mode investments as a financial portfolio to optimize returns and manage systemic risk.
ideas.sourceTitle: Transport Investment and Economic Development

ideas.title: Relax the assumption of fixed environmental limits
ideas.description: Investigate if technological innovation can expand or redefine planetary boundaries.

ideas.description: Focus rehabilitation efforts on a single, most critical railway line as a pilot for national reform.

ideas.title: Validating configurational models of building layouts with multi-modal cognitive and physiological stress assessment
ideas.description: This research applies configurational modelling (space syntax) to indoor architectural networks to generate objective predictions about spatial complexity and navigational difficulty. It then challenges the sole reliance on self-report questionnaires by testing these predictions against a multi-method assessment combining self-report, performance-based cognitive tasks (e.g., wayfinding accuracy, memory load), and physiological stress markers (e.g., electrodermal activity, heart rate variability) collected from participants navigating these environments. The synergy creates a methodologically robust paradigm that can disentangle whether stressful or disorienting experiences in buildings are due to verifiable spatial properties or individual subjective biases, offering evidence-based guidelines for design that supports cognitive ease and reduces physiological stress.

ideas.title: Challenge homogeneity of the 'elderly' category
ideas.description: Research could segment the elderly population by more than age, such as health status or lifestyle.
ideas.sourceTitle: Quality of life for the elderly: the transport dimension

ideas.title: Scale up to couple or household quality of life
ideas.description: Investigate quality of life as a dyadic or household construct, not just an individual perception.
ideas.sourceTitle: Let's Ask Them: A National Survey of Definitions of Quality of Life and Its Enhancement among People Aged 65 and Over

ideas.title: Update country assessments with new data annually
ideas.description: Establish a dynamic monitoring system to track national progress toward the four thresholds over time.
ideas.sourceTitle: Sustainable development: Our Common Future revisited

ideas.title: Triangulating Self-Report with Behavioral Trace Data and Implicit Association
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that self-report questionnaires are sufficient by proposing a methodological triangulation inspired by digital archaeology and historical analysis. In those fields, scholars overcome the biases and incompleteness of official records (analogous to self-reports) by systematically combining them with material artifacts (behavioral trace data) and spatial/structural evidence (implicit measures). Adapted to psychological or social science research, the solution is to design studies that concurrently collect: 1) traditional self-report questionnaires, 2) passive behavioral trace data from digital devices or environmental monitors, and 3) performance-based implicit association or cognitive task data. The convergence or divergence among these three distinct streams of evidence provides a more robust, multi-faceted understanding of the construct, moving beyond simply validating self-report with another direct assessment (like an interview) to building a composite picture from fundamentally different types of data.

ideas.title: Inverse Network Design via Metabolic Control Theory
ideas.description: This research seed expands the original idea of designing networks for target flows by drawing an analogy from Systems Biology, specifically Metabolic Engineering. In that field, the analogous problem is designing a metabolic network (by modifying enzyme levels or gene knockouts) to achieve a desired distribution of chemical fluxes for optimal bioproduction. This is solved using inverse flux balance analysis and optimization techniques like OptKnock. Adapted to the urban context, the research would treat desired traffic or pedestrian movement patterns as a target flux distribution. The solution involves developing a novel computational inverse model that, starting from these target flows, solves for the street network configuration (layout, connectivity, capacities) and associated traffic control parameters that would induce these flows as a user-optimal equilibrium. This transforms the classic traffic assignment problem into an inverse design problem, with potential applications in urban planning and logistics network design.

ideas.title: Empirically distinguish cognitive from network effects
ideas.description: Apply the new method to conclusively separate psychological navigational choices from emergent statistical network effects.

ideas.description: Find the transport mix that minimizes DALYs while staying within a strict carbon cap.
ideas.sourceTitle: Public health benefits of strategies to reduce greenhouse-gas emissions: urban land transport.

ideas.title: Invert the focus from enhancement to prevention of loss

ideas.description: Calculate the maximum feasible human development attainable while strictly respecting all four sustainability thresholds.
ideas.sourceTitle: Sustainable development: Our Common Future revisited

ideas.title: Compare different equity-regarding social welfare functions
ideas.description: Empirically analyze how policy rankings change under various specifications of the equity-regarding SWF.
ideas.sourceTitle: Distributive justice and equity in transportation

ideas.title: Dual: Model maximizing welfare within constraints
ideas.description: Formulate an optimization problem to maximize social welfare subject to equity and environmental limits.
ideas.sourceTitle: The Imperatives of Sustainable Development

ideas.title: Multi-system mapping of resilience and vulnerability to distinguish adaptive capacity from systemic burden
ideas.description: This research program moves beyond a simple focus on individual resilience factors to a dynamic, multi-level analysis. It investigates how individual capacities interact with relational support, community resources, and societal structures to produce long-term outcomes. The design explicitly identifies contexts where promoting 'resilience' is inappropriate because the primary drivers of poor outcomes are systemic failures or ongoing adversity. The goal is to generate a nuanced map that informs both individual-level interventions and necessary policy or structural changes to genuinely support survivor well-being.

ideas.title: Invert focus from outcomes to ex-ante opportunities
ideas.description: Shift analysis from distributed outcomes to the equitable distribution of risks and opportunities before policies are implemented.
ideas.sourceTitle: Distributive justice and equity in transportation

ideas.title: Longitudinal Multi-Method Tracking of Dynamic Meaning-Making in Cancer Experience
ideas.description: This study uses a longitudinal design to capture the dynamic and often non-linear process of finding meaning after a cancer diagnosis. It combines serial qualitative interviews, conducted at key clinical and personal timepoints, with ecological momentary assessment (EMA) via a smartphone app to collect real-time data on emotions and coping. This multi-method approach triangulates reflective narratives with in-the-moment experiences, reducing recall bias and the social desirability of reporting 'growth.' The analysis explicitly codes for contradictions, ambivalence, and negative shifts, providing a more robust and nuanced understanding of the personal meaning of cancer over time.

ideas.title: All survivors are at equal risk long-term
ideas.description: Invert to assume most survivors are resilient and study factors promoting resilience, not just risk.
ideas.sourceTitle: A multidimensional model of the quality of life in older age

ideas.title: A Volumetric-Grounded Multi-Layer Network for Configurational Analysis
ideas.description: This research creates a hybrid analytical system that directly links volumetric urban models to configurational analysis. First, it constructs a detailed 3D voxel or surface model representing navigable space, visual permeability, and functional zones. Second, a rule-based abstraction engine translates this volume into a multi-graph network, where edges and nodes carry attributes of width, vertical connectivity, and visual exposure derived from the source model. Finally, this enriched network enables the computation of both traditional and novel configurational measures, ensuring analytical rigor while fundamentally grounding the analysis in area-based spatial reality.

ideas.title: Inverse problem: Design optimally equitable policies
ideas.description: Use the PPPA framework not just to evaluate, but to computationally search for policies that maximize equity-regarding SWF.
ideas.sourceTitle: Distributive justice and equity in transportation

ideas.title: Apply microsimulation models from public health
ideas.description: Use established microsimulation techniques to generate the lifetime population profiles required for PPPA.
ideas.sourceTitle: Distributive justice and equity in transportation

ideas.title: Solve the inverse problem: optimal hub location
ideas.description: Given a growth target, determine the optimal locations for new transport hubs, not just investing in existing ones.
ideas.sourceTitle: Transport investment and the promotion of economic growth

ideas.title: Multi-agent reinforcement learning for emergent navigation and adaptive flow dynamics
ideas.description: This research employs an agent-based model where individual navigators are not programmed with static cognitive rules but are instead autonomous learning agents using reinforcement learning. These agents interact within a simulated spatial network, learning and adapting their routing policies based on personal experience, environmental feedback (like congestion), and potentially shared, noisy information. The core analysis investigates the emergent aggregate flow patterns, stability conditions, and system-level efficiency that arise from this decentralized learning process, directly comparing them to both classical equilibrium predictions and real-world observational data to validate the adaptive mechanics.

ideas.title: Integrate PPPA with existing cost-benefit analysis
ideas.description: Develop a hybrid analytical framework that formally combines equity assessment from PPPA with traditional cost-benefit metrics.
ideas.sourceTitle: Distributive justice and equity in transportation

ideas.title: Narrative-Informed Agent-Based Modeling of Illness Experience and Healthcare Navigation
ideas.description: This research direction first uses in-depth narrative interviews with cancer patients to uncover the personal meanings, emotional logics, and cognitive heuristics that guide their decisions (e.g., seeking treatment, interpreting symptoms, navigating care systems). These qualitative insights are then formally encoded as the behavioral rules for agents in a computational model. The agent-based simulation generates aggregate flows of patient navigation and outcomes, which can be analyzed to reveal systemic bottlenecks or unexpected emergent phenomena. The key innovation is closing the loop: simulation outcomes can be presented back to participants or new cohorts in qualitative interviews to refine the rules, creating a iterative, mixed-methods framework for understanding and intervening in complex healthcare ecosystems.

ideas.title: Challenge separability of health, longevity, and income
ideas.description: Investigate how interactions between health, longevity, and income in a life history affect equity judgments.
ideas.sourceTitle: Distributive justice and equity in transportation

ideas.title: Refine measurement of lifetime health-longevity-income histories
ideas.description: Improve data collection and modeling techniques for constructing the individual histories that form population profiles.
ideas.sourceTitle: Distributive justice and equity in transportation

ideas.title: A Coupled Statistical-Mechanical and Agent-Based Framework for Strategic Flow Emergence in Networks
ideas.description: This research develops a hybrid framework to model the emergence of network flows from both structural constraints and strategic agent behavior. It couples a statistical mechanics ensemble, which provides a probabilistic description of flow configurations based on network topology and generalized costs, with a population of boundedly rational agents that make adaptive routing decisions. The coupling is formalized through a dynamical feedback where agent choices act as perturbations driving the system away from structural equilibrium, and the resulting macroscopic flow patterns (e.g., congestion) are computed and fed back into agent decision models. This approach captures path-dependence, learning, and the emergence of non-equilibrium steady states that pure structural models cannot predict, significantly improving realism for applications like traffic, data routing, and economic networks.

ideas.title: Use gamification from digital engagement
ideas.description: Incorporate game elements like points and badges into PTPs to sustain engagement.
ideas.sourceTitle: Cities, mobility and climate change

ideas.title: Constrained Co-Design: Generating Feasible Network Families for Target Flow Patterns
ideas.description: This research develops a system that treats the network inverse problem as a constrained, multi-objective co-design challenge. It first validates target flow patterns for internal consistency and physical realizability against fundamental principles. The core engine then generates a diverse family of legally and topologically feasible street network configurations that collectively approximate the desired flows, optimizing simultaneously for infrastructure cost, robustness, and flow fidelity. The result is a tunable design space—a Pareto frontier—presenting planners with actionable, real-world network options rather than a single, likely infeasible, mathematical solution.

ideas.title: Use systems dynamics to model dimension interactions
ideas.description: Model the four dimensions as a coupled system to simulate feedbacks and tipping points.
ideas.sourceTitle: Sustainable development: Our Common Future revisited

ideas.title: Challenge national cultural homogeneity in definitions
ideas.description: Test if the British findings generalize or if cultural values fundamentally reshape quality of life hierarchies.
ideas.sourceTitle: Let's Ask Them: A National Survey of Definitions of Quality of Life and Its Enhancement among People Aged 65 and Over

ideas.title: Disentangling Cognitive from Network Effects via Ecological Niche Modeling Techniques
ideas.description: This research adapts the solution from ecology's niche modeling problem to empirically distinguish cognitive from network effects. In ecology, species distribution models (SDMs) disentangle a species' intrinsic physiological preferences (fundamental niche) from its actual, constrained distribution in the wild (realized niche). Analogously, we propose to model an individual's 'cognitive niche'—their ideal navigational choices based on psychological preferences measured in controlled, network-agnostic experiments (e.g., lab studies of information foraging). This model generates a predicted optimal behavioral path. By comparing this prediction to the individual's observed 'realized' path within a real, complex network (e.g., website navigation, citation hopping, social media feeds), the systematic discrepancy can be rigorously quantified. This mismatch is then attributed to emergent statistical network effects, such as link topology, recommendation algorithms, or community boundaries, providing a conclusive empirical method for separation.

ideas.title: Use spatial econometrics to model spillovers
ideas.description: Incorporate spatial lag and error models to account for growth interdependence between neighboring provinces.
ideas.sourceTitle: Transport investment and the promotion of economic growth

ideas.title: Adopt multi-criteria decision analysis frameworks
ideas.description: Formalize equity vs. efficiency trade-offs using structured MCDA methods common in environmental management.
ideas.sourceTitle: Distributive justice and equity in transportation

ideas.title: A Triangulation Framework for Causal Attribution in Network Navigation
ideas.description: This research employs a multi-method causal framework to rigorously separate cognitive strategies from emergent network effects in navigation tasks. It combines controlled laboratory experiments measuring individual search heuristics with parallel, large-scale analyses of digital trace data on real networks. Crucially, it uses agent-based models, where simulated agents with experimentally-validated cognitive rules navigate real and randomized network topologies, to isolate the unique and interactive causal contributions of psychology and network structure.

ideas.title: Challenge universality of social relationships as primary
ideas.description: Test if health or independence supersedes social relationships as the core quality of life factor for specific subgroups.
ideas.sourceTitle: Let's Ask Them: A National Survey of Definitions of Quality of Life and Its Enhancement among People Aged 65 and Over

ideas.title: Use qualitative narrative analysis techniques
ideas.description: Apply in-depth interview methods to understand the personal meaning of cancer and growth.
