ideas.sourceTitle: Let's Ask Them: A National Survey of Definitions of Quality of Life and Its Enhancement among People Aged 65 and Over

ideas.title: Adapt Just-In-Time Adaptive Intervention (JITAI) framework from behavioral medicine for PTSS symptom management
ideas.description: This idea adapts the Just-In-Time Adaptive Intervention (JITAI) framework, a solution from behavioral medicine for conditions like diabetes management, to the problem of capturing and managing real-time PTSS fluctuations. The analogous solution uses Ecological Momentary Assessment (EMA) data to trigger timely, automated, and context-aware micro-interventions via smartphone when a user is at high risk. Adapted to this research context, we propose using intensive longitudinal smartphone sampling (EMA + passive sensing) of PTSS symptoms, affect, and context to develop a predictive model of symptom escalation. This model would drive a JITAI system that delivers personalized, in-the-moment psychosocial micro-interventions (e.g., brief grounding exercises, coping statements) precisely when a survivor is predicted to be entering a high-distress state, transforming assessment into a dynamic, preventive intervention tool.

ideas.title: Intervention development for high-risk survivor subgroups
ideas.description: Create targeted psychological interventions for survivors with specific risk factors like young age or low education.
ideas.sourceTitle: A multidimensional model of the quality of life in older age

ideas.title: Use multi-criteria decision analysis for dimension weighting

ideas.title: Engineering Resilience: Identifying Protective Factors in Trauma Survivors
ideas.description: This idea inverts the assumption that all survivors are at equal long-term risk by instead assuming most possess inherent resilience, analogous to how materials science studies why some materials withstand stress rather than why all fail. Drawing from the analogous solution in materials science and structural engineering—which identifies microstructural properties and design principles that confer resilience—this research adapts that approach. It would prospectively study trauma survivors to characterize the specific psychological, social, and neurobiological 'protective factors' and 'design principles' that promote resilience, moving beyond risk factors to build a foundational model for actively fostering resilience in at-risk populations.

ideas.title: Modeling the Temporal Dynamics and Causal Contours of PTSS, PTG, and Support Systems
ideas.description: This redesigned study moves beyond static correlation by using a multi-wave longitudinal design to model the temporal and potentially causal relationships between PTSS, PTG, and support. It applies Graphical Vector Autoregressive (GVAR) network models to intensive longitudinal data (e.g., daily diaries) to establish temporal precedence and Granger causality among specific symptoms and perceptions. These dynamic findings are then validated and contextualized using Cross-Lagged Panel Network (CLPN) models on a larger sample with longer intervals, while explicitly incorporating latent variable models to account for measurement error and shared underlying factors, thereby yielding a more robust and actionable map of the system's evolution over time.

ideas.title: Employ conjoint analysis for priority weighting
ideas.description: Use choice-based experiments to rigorously quantify trade-offs older adults make between quality of life domains.
ideas.sourceTitle: Let's Ask Them: A National Survey of Definitions of Quality of Life and Its Enhancement among People Aged 65 and Over

ideas.title: Dual: Analyze inequity sources, not just outcomes
ideas.description: Use the profile data to identify structural drivers of inequity, complementing the outcome-focused PPPA.

ideas.title: Apply network analysis to symptom co-occurrence
ideas.description: Use psychometric network methods to model interactions between specific PTSS, PTG, and support items.

ideas.title: Use inequality indices from economics
ideas.description: Incorporate Gini coefficients, Atkinson indices, or other inequality metrics as components of the social welfare function.
ideas.sourceTitle: Distributive justice and equity in transportation

ideas.title: Investigating Social Support as a Buffer and Pathway: A Network Resilience Model for Cancer Trauma Outcomes
ideas.description: This research adapts solutions from ecosystem ecology and resilience theory to investigate the mechanisms linking social support to PTSS and PTG following cancer trauma. In ecology, a system's response to a major disturbance is analyzed through its network structure—how connections, modularity, and keystone elements determine resistance and recovery. The analogous solution is to map the supportive interaction network to predict outcomes. Adapted to this context, the study would conceptualize an individual's perceived social support as a dynamic, multi-dimensional network (e.g., emotional, informational, instrumental ties) and employ network analysis techniques. It would test how specific structural properties of this support network (e.g., connectivity, tie strength, functional redundancy) mediate or moderate the trauma response, acting as either a buffer against pathological collapse (PTSS) or a scaffold enabling positive reorganization and growth (PTG).

ideas.title: Modeling PTSS/PTG Trajectories with Latent Class Growth Analysis and Ecological Momentary Assessment
ideas.description: This study adapts methods from developmental epidemiology to track Post-Traumatic Stress Symptoms (PTSS) and Post-Traumatic Growth (PTG) from adolescence into later adulthood. By employing a longitudinal cohort design with repeated measures, we will use Latent Class Growth Analysis (LCGA) or Growth Mixture Modeling (GMM) to identify distinct, heterogeneous trajectories of PTSS and PTG co-evolution, moving beyond single average trends. The adapted solution integrates Ecological Momentary Assessment (EMA) during selected assessment waves to capture dynamic, real-time processes (e.g., affect, rumination, social interactions) that may predict or characterize membership in the different long-term trajectory classes, thereby linking micro-level daily experiences to macro-level developmental pathways.

ideas.title: Use statistical mechanics for network flow emergence
ideas.description: Apply methods from statistical physics to model emergent flow patterns from network structure alone.
ideas.sourceTitle: Configurational Modelling of Urban Movement Networks

ideas.title: Agent-Based Modeling of Intervention Pathways for High-Risk Survivor Subgroups
ideas.description: This research synthesizes targeted intervention development with agent-based modeling to create a novel simulation platform. It models individual survivors from high-risk subgroups (e.g., young age, low education) as agents endowed with cognitive-behavioral rules that govern their response to psychological interventions and their navigation through post-trauma recovery pathways. By simulating large populations, the model can generate and analyze aggregate mental health flow patterns, revealing unintended consequences, critical intervention timing, and optimal resource allocation. This allows for the virtual prototyping and iterative refinement of interventions tailored to complex, real-world survivor ecosystems before costly clinical trials.

ideas.title: Comparative Survivorship Burden Analysis Using a Multi-System Framework
ideas.description: This research idea adapts a multi-system assessment framework from ecosystem ecology to compare cancer survivors. In ecology, scientists compare the recovery of different ecosystems (like forests and coral reefs) by profiling multiple functional domains (nutrient cycling, biodiversity, etc.) rather than a single metric. Applying this analogical solution, the study would directly compare survivors of hematological versus solid tumors by collecting large-sample data on several interconnected survivorship domains: psychological distress, cognitive function, social role participation, financial toxicity, and chronic physical symptoms. The goal is to move beyond a single-outcome comparison to generate and contrast holistic 'burden profiles,' analyzing both the severity within each domain and the patterns of correlation between domains across the two cancer-type groups. This could reveal whether one survivor population experiences a more syndromic (tightly coupled) or diffuse pattern of post-treatment challenges, informing more tailored intervention strategies.

ideas.title: Challenge welfarism by incorporating non-utility metrics
ideas.description: Relax the welfarist assumption by integrating rights, capabilities, or procedural justice directly into the equity assessment.
ideas.sourceTitle: Distributive justice and equity in transportation

ideas.title: A Dynamic Systems Approach to Caregiver Stress and Growth: Modeling Trajectories and Tipping Points
ideas.description: This research adapts models from ecology on ecosystem response to catastrophic disturbance to investigate the dual problem of post-traumatic stress and post-traumatic growth in parents of childhood cancer survivors. The analogous solution involves longitudinal tracking and dynamic systems modeling to identify states, trajectories, and tipping points within a complex system. The adapted idea treats the caregiver's psychological world as an ecosystem, using repeated measures and computational modeling to map how stress and growth co-evolve over time, aiming to pinpoint critical buffering factors and intervention windows that push the system toward a resilient, growth-oriented state rather than a chronic distress state.

ideas.title: Apply planetary boundaries framework quantitatively
ideas.description: Integrate quantified planetary boundary science directly into the environmental limits imperative.
ideas.sourceTitle: The Imperatives of Sustainable Development

ideas.title: Challenge single economic center assumption
ideas.description: Model multiple, competing economic centers (e.g., Beijing, Shanghai, Guangzhou) instead of a single coastal center.
ideas.sourceTitle: Transport investment and the promotion of economic growth

ideas.title: Adapting Oral History Life-Course Mapping to Chart Personal Narratives of Cancer and Growth
ideas.description: This idea adapts a methodological solution from oral history—the life-course timeline mapping interview—to the context of understanding the personal meaning of cancer and growth. In oral history, interviewees collaboratively construct a visual timeline of life events with the researcher, which then serves as a concrete reference to explore narrative connections, causal attributions, and the subjective significance of turning points. Adapted here, cancer patients would create a combined biographical and medical timeline during an in-depth interview. The researcher would then use this participant-generated map not just as a data source but as an analytical tool to guide the conversation, enabling a deeper narrative analysis of how the individual weaves the experience of illness into their life story, constructs meaning, and perceives post-traumatic growth. This method transfers a technique for eliciting structured yet fluid personal narratives from one field to another, aiming to capture the nuanced, temporal, and causal dimensions of meaning-making that standard interview protocols might miss.

ideas.title: Scale down to hyper-local neighborhood plans
ideas.description: Create micro-PTPs tailored to specific residential blocks or workplace campuses.

ideas.title: Scale up to multi-center international cohort
ideas.description: Expand recruitment to multiple countries to study cultural influences on PTSS and PTG.
ideas.sourceTitle: A multidimensional model of the quality of life in older age

ideas.title: Standardized Cultural Phenotyping Framework for Multi-Center PTSS/PTG Research
ideas.description: This idea expands the original multi-center international cohort by applying a solution from human genomics. In genetics, international consortia face the challenge of combining data from biobanks in different countries with varying healthcare systems and data practices. Their solution is to use standardized phenotyping algorithms that transform local data into consistent, pre-defined traits for cross-cohort analysis. Adapted to research on Post-Traumatic Stress Symptoms (PTSS) and Post-Traumatic Growth (PTG), this involves creating a 'Cultural Phenotyping Framework.' Prior to data aggregation, each participating international center would run a shared, validated computational pipeline on their locally-collected data. This pipeline would algorithmically derive core study variables—such as standardized PTSS/PTG constructs and quantifiable cultural dimension scores—from the raw inputs. This method ensures that the variables being compared across cultures are truly equivalent, mitigating the confounding effects of differing assessment tools, clinical practices, and cultural interpretations of scale items. The result is a robust, harmonized dataset capable of precisely disentangling the influence of culture on trauma outcomes from mere measurement heterogeneity.

ideas.title: Question if Asian cooperation is the top priority
ideas.description: Analyze if deepening ties with European neighbors offers greater economic returns than the stated Asia priority.
ideas.sourceTitle: Transport Investment and Economic Development

ideas.title: Mechanism-Targeted Adaptive Interventions for Survivors Using Dynamic Risk Stratification
ideas.description: This research develops personalized psychological interventions by first using a data-driven, transdiagnostic framework to identify survivor subgroups based on shared underlying mechanisms of dysfunction (e.g., reward processing deficits, avoidance patterns). It then creates a suite of modular, evidence-based treatment components that directly target these specific mechanisms. The intervention system is adaptive, using ongoing performance and symptom monitoring to algorithmically adjust the type and dosage of treatment modules in real time for each individual. This ensures the intervention remains precisely targeted to the individual's current clinical needs, moving beyond static, demographic-based categories to improve efficacy and efficiency.

ideas.title: Beyond the Primary Lesion: A Network Analysis of Post-Trauma Psychopathology
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that Post-Traumatic Stress Disorder (PTSD) is the primary negative psychological outcome following trauma by drawing an analogy from cardiology's evolution beyond the myocardial infarction model. In cardiology, solving the analogous problem involved moving from a focus on a single critical event to understanding systemic syndromes and comorbidities. Adapting this solution, the research would conceptualize trauma as a systemic insult and use methods like network analysis to investigate the shared and distinct disruptions in core affective, cognitive, and threat-processing systems that probabilistically give rise to a range of sequelae, including depression, anxiety disorders, and PTSD. This shifts the question from 'which disorder is primary?' to 'how does trauma disrupt the system, and what factors determine the emergent clinical picture?'

ideas.title: Precision phenotyping for intervention tailoring: Using multi-modal assessment to define and target high-risk survivor subgroups
ideas.description: This research direction challenges the sufficiency of self-report for identifying high-risk survivor subgroups by integrating it with clinical interviews and physiological stress markers (e.g., heart rate variability, cortisol) to create precise, multi-modal phenotypic profiles. These profiles are then used to define distinct, empirically-derived high-risk subgroups, moving beyond broad demographic categories like 'young age'. The core innovation is the development of targeted interventions specifically designed for these mechanistically-defined phenotypes, allowing for treatment matching based on an individual's unique combination of subjective distress, clinical presentation, and physiological dysregulation.

ideas.title: Adapting Single-Subject Experimental Design from Behavioral Psychology to Intensive PTSS/PTG Case Studies
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from Behavioral Psychology, where single-subject experimental designs are used to establish causal relationships within an individual, to the study of extreme post-traumatic stress (PTSS) or post-traumatic growth (PTG). The analogous solution involves intensive, continuous data collection on a single case while systematically observing responses to manipulated or naturally varying conditions. Adapted to this context, researchers would conduct a detailed idiographic study of an individual with extreme scores, employing high-frequency ecological momentary assessments, physiological monitoring, and behavioral tracking over an extended period. By analyzing the temporal sequences and contingencies between events, internal states, and symptom or growth expressions, this method moves beyond correlation to reveal the functional, person-specific architecture of trauma response, identifying unique mechanisms that drive extreme outcomes.

ideas.title: Empirically Mapping the Primary Adverse Post-Trauma Psychopathology Spectra
ideas.description: This study moves beyond challenging one disorder as 'primary' to instead empirically identify the core adverse outcome dimensions following trauma. Using a large, prospective cohort assessed at multiple time points, it applies advanced computational psychopathology methods—such as Gaussian graphical network modeling and latent profile analysis—to high-fidelity symptom data across traditional diagnostic boundaries. The goal is to reveal the latent structure of post-trauma psychopathology, determining whether impairment is best explained by a central hub symptom, specific symptom clusters, or particular dynamic interactions between symptoms like anhedonia, hypervigilance, and negative affect. This data-driven approach aims to replace a priori diagnostic debates with an evidence-based taxonomy of primary post-trauma adversity.

ideas.title: Adapt a multi-system biomarker panel from environmental toxicology to assess psychobiological stress load
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from environmental toxicology, where the biological impact of complex, diffuse exposures is assessed using a multi-system biomarker panel rather than a single compound. The analogous solution involves measuring a suite of biomarkers across different biological response pathways (e.g., oxidative stress, inflammation, epigenetics) to calculate a composite 'biological load' score. Adapted to psychoneuroimmunology, the research idea is to develop and validate a standardized multi-system biomarker panel for 'psychobiological stress load.' This panel would integrate synchronized measures from the HPA axis (e.g., cortisol, DHEA-S), innate immune system (e.g., CRP, IL-6, TNF-α), metabolic function, and oxidative stress to generate a composite score. This methodological transfer provides a more comprehensive, systems-level biological signature linking chronic psychological stress to cumulative biological dysregulation, moving beyond single-marker approaches.

ideas.title: Precision Intervention Engineering for Survivor Subgroups via Biomarker-Guided Modules
ideas.description: This research adapts the precision medicine model from oncology to create targeted psychological interventions for high-risk survivor subgroups. In oncology, solutions involve screening for specific molecular biomarkers to guide the selection of targeted therapies. Analogously, this project proposes first using biobehavioral phenotyping (e.g., stress physiology, neuroimaging, genetics) to identify precise mechanistic pathways to psychopathology within survivor groups defined by factors like young age or low education. The adapted solution is to then engineer modular intervention components that directly target these mechanisms, moving beyond broad demographic categories to a biomarker-guided, mechanism-focused approach for more potent and personalized survivor mental healthcare.

ideas.title: Algorithmic Triage for Efficient Multi-Method Validation
ideas.description: This approach implements a sequential, two-stage assessment system to overcome the impracticality of blanket multi-method testing. First, all participants complete a carefully calibrated self-report questionnaire designed for high sensitivity. A machine learning algorithm then analyzes these responses to identify a high-priority subgroup for whom self-report may be unreliable or symptom severity is ambiguous. Only this targeted subgroup proceeds to the second stage, which involves a structured clinical interview and concurrent physiological stress measurement. This design ensures the resource-intensive methods are deployed efficiently, maximizing validation power while maintaining feasibility for large-scale studies or clinical screening.

ideas.title: Resilience Signatures: A Multi-Omic and Psychosocial Study of Cancer Trauma Resistance
ideas.description: This research adapts the solution from HIV research on 'Elite Controllers' to the problem of psychological resilience following cancer trauma. Just as virologists identified host factors protecting against AIDS progression, we will identify biopsychosocial factors protecting against PTSS. We will define a cohort of 'Cancer Trauma Elite Controllers' (patients with high trauma exposure but minimal PTSS) and conduct a multi-omic (genomic, epigenomic, transcriptomic) and deep psychosocial comparison with a matched cohort of typical 'progressors' (patients with significant PTSS). The goal is to map a composite 'resilience signature' encompassing genetic variants (e.g., in stress-related neurotransmitter systems), epigenetic modifications (e.g., in glucocorticoid receptor genes), stable biomarkers (e.g., inflammatory profiles, HPA axis function), and psychological traits or social resources, thereby revealing the integrated mechanisms of natural psychological resistance.
